主持人： It demonstrates the power of a founder company even being post-public — Vlad is doing AI trading stuff. He's leaned the most into it.

Josh： I'm just blown away by what they've done since I've been gone. I left at the end of 2019. It's turned into this incredible multi-platform — no longer just trading. Retirement, credit card, banking components. Vlad has done an incredible job navigating the seas. They went through bumpy times too — crazy downtimes in the stock market, GameStop felt like it might be existential for the brand. But they came back to their core values: great design, great product, trust for the user, something people are proud to talk about and share. They've really been able to win people back and grow a ton.

TikTok / Musical.ly 的故事

主持人： Okay, so we've established Robinhood was a referral program. Now talk about Musical.ly / TikTok. Not everybody may know the connection. They're the other great exception — anytime we say "paid doesn't really work at scale," people say "but TikTok."

Josh： This is a crazy multi-arc story. Musical.ly was a small team in China trying to build for the Western markets. They were actually starting trying to build an education app — hadn't really worked. They had seen some fun things happening around lip-syncing, and decided they'd build a little lip-sync app.

They put it on the App Store, called it "lip sync," and were seeing spikes every week. Alex, the founder of Musical.ly (and who ran a lot of TikTok), told me they were so confused. They finally found out the spikes on Thursday night were because of when Lip Sync Battle aired on MTV. Lip Sync Battle would run, people would go type "lip sync" into the App Store, download Musical.ly, and start creating lip-syncs.

Musical.ly designed their product in two really smart ways. First, it was really easy to take your video and post it elsewhere. You could create a great lip-sync video and post it to Instagram. Second, they curated a feed of the great lip-sync videos. When you'd open the app, you'd swipe through, add some friends, and create your video. They might post it on Instagram.

That loop was: create something you couldn't create anywhere else, then post it out to Instagram and your friends. You'd start following your friends, see what they had posted alongside other really cool stuff being curated.

They found out there were a bunch of B+ stars on Instagram who weren't quite getting enough credibility — only posting pictures (Instagram video was just coming out at the time). They were like, "let me use Musical.ly to create some cool content for my Instagram feed." And on Musical.ly, they became the number one stars, the A-listers, because they were getting featured in the feed.

Now you had this loop: influencers posting tons of stuff on Instagram, people seeing Musical.ly on Instagram, having a better experience in the feed, talking about it with friends. It all started to compound. The other secret was there was always a little Musical.ly bug at the bottom of the Instagram video. You could download the video — unheard of at the time. Create the video in Musical.ly, download it, upload it to Instagram, and it would keep the Musical.ly bug. People weren't cutting that out. It created this great loop.

The company got really big — tens and tens of millions of users daily, hundreds had tried it. Then they started thinking whether to keep going independently, raise more, or sell. Got an incredible offer from ByteDance. ByteDance had a product, Douyin, that was very large in China — they had modeled some after Musical.ly, had their own invention, had a great AI team. They were like, "we can help Musical.ly scale even more worldwide." So they bought Musical.ly.

They spent about six months tinkering, rebuilding the product, moving Musical.ly and Douyin into a shared codebase. Then they relaunched it with ads, with dollars — because to get from here to where we want to go, we need to pour a lot more into it. They were probably one of the biggest spenders on Facebook, Twitter, every social media to get people to download TikTok at that time.

But the product loop was so good that everybody retained. You'd sign up, and with a couple swipes you were deeply personalized. Your friends already on Musical.ly were coming back trying new things. It was really energizing — the whole loop worked. It still took them pouring a lot of money in to get distribution, but the retention really worked.

I joke at the same time, Quibi was pouring similar amounts of money to get people to download Quibi — which didn't totally work.

主持人： Didn't work as well.

Josh： So is the lesson — if you have a highly retained working loop, you can do paid and it's okay?

Josh： I have no problem with people doing paid if they understand it. Paid to bring in people who will become very good retained users and spread the word can be a great catalyst. What you can't do is spend a lot of money to burn 10 people to find the one who actually stuck — and think that scales. That only gets more expensive.

消费者付费意愿与推理成本

主持人： Adjacent to this is a debate we've had: it's amazing that consumers are willing to pay for software in a big way. Top ChatGPT SKU is $250 a month. Top Grok SKU is $300 a month. Gemini Ultra I think is $300. Consumers are paying for software. On the other hand, because there's no zero marginal cost of distribution — inference costs money — you can't really build a mass market free product without a massive balance sheet. Is it better, worse? Is it just changing the shape of startups?

Josh： This willingness to pay and necessity to pay is definitely shifting. 15-20 years ago everything was shifting to the cloud, and the startup cost became much less. You didn't have to buy a bunch of servers and rack them. Sure, serving cost a little money in bandwidth and compute, but relatively low for what you were getting.

Now if you're running tons of inference, you need your customers willing to pay for it. They are. The ability to pay in apps is so much easier than it's ever been. Getting payments is easy if you're providing value.

I think it hurts companies that need massive network effects — because part of the benefit of massive network effects was free distribution to everybody. I haven't totally figured out what the balance is, but that's what I'm excited to work through over the next few years with various startups.

主持人： It seems like one thing that could really change that is if iPhones get really powerful GPUs.

Josh： We're going to be figuring out, with the models you can run on iPhones or laptops, how to push more of the less-complex inference onto device to lower the cost. There's no reason that everything you're ever trying to do — refining a title, rewriting a paragraph, answering basic questions — can't use models that are incredibly powerful on the device. Then you only send the more complex stuff up. From both enterprise and consumer sides, really smart founders will optimize that.

主持人： I know Karpathy has done a bunch of work, a few folks have shown you can actually train and run models on 386s and 486s. There's a lot of good old-fashioned engineering that will push more and more to the edge.

实验室会赢一切吗？

主持人： One topic we discuss a lot: are the labs just going to win it all? Should we all just go home? It feels like we have a version of this conversation every product cycle. What's your view?

Josh： A funny way to think about this is — 20 years ago, everybody was like, "won't Google just do it?" Then Google could do everything. Certainly Google has expanded in a bunch of categories. But there's a ton of startups that have grown and become massive companies since then.

15 years ago, 2010 era, everybody was like, "well, isn't Apple just going to do it all? Mobile apps will all be their own apps." We had this great world of mobile-enabled apps and social apps we could have never imagined.

About 10 years ago, I kind of started thinking that too — I wasn't sure if you could truly build another consumer startup from scratch in these networks that are now 1 billion plus users. It's part of why I actually left venture to go build again. I went to Robinhood, which led me over to Apple. I said, "gosh, I'm not sure if you can build something big enough in pure consumer areas."

I'm not sure if I was wrong. ChatGPT obviously found it. I had invested in Discord. We had just sold Musical.ly. Those were going to make it through. I joined Robinhood — that one's going to make it through. But starting from scratch, you have to get enough momentum to get to that billion-user scale. I wasn't so sure. If I didn't really believe I could find that path in venture, I was going to work on them instead. Part of why I went to Apple was to learn and work at the biggest scale and try to deliver value to everybody's pocket.

But right now, I think the world is so open again. The main chatbots, the main assistants, are going to do a lot for us — we're going to rely on them for a lot of things. But we're going to expect everything to work like them. And we're going to rewire so much else of what we do in life — our services, the way we get things done — that the opportunities to fill all those areas of our lives, help us connect, help us get information, help us explore, are not just going to go to "there's only two products we ever use."

创业公司能做但大公司做不了的事

主持人： I totally agree. There are ways startups can play that incumbents and labs can't. Companion is such a great example — it touches on everything that every committee at a big tech company doesn't want you to do. There's disagreement, probabilistic behavior, sexuality — all these ways you can interact with a product that are very human. At really big scale, it's hard to ship things like that.

Josh： Having been part of one of the biggest companies, learning to ship things that matter and make the world better is kind of the fundamental value of what we do — and the fundamental value of what you can do at that scale. But when I think about companion products and entertainment and how AI can take us on these journeys — it's incredible how much different it can be than everything we've had in the past. We've never had an experience like that.

On the other hand, it's a high level of responsibility to be very careful — with kids, with safety, with sexuality, with letting people who might be going through mental challenges feel they're getting something served through a product that isn't bringing them back to humanity or helping them get the help they need. We have to find that balance.

But once you accept that's the baseline — there is so much to explore that not every product wants to be. Lots of products don't want to be your best friend. If you've used the new Siri — it's incredible, but it's very much trying to help you get things done. It's designed to help you get things done with the experiences and content you already have, what it knows about you, what it can access in the world. It doesn't need to be sycophantic. It doesn't need to say "you look great today" and create that personality.

But there are other experiences where you might want opinions on a wardrobe and how you dress, give you tips, treat you a little bit more in that human relationship. That's a very different product.

不同 AI 产品的不同灵魂

主持人： If you unbundle the two — there's a product that's just about getting things done, almost has no soul by design. We need that. But then there are products that might mirror things we do in the rest of the world. I might join a volleyball team because I'm looking for friends, and volleyball is a level of indirection to building new adult male friendships. There will also be companion products that look like that.

Josh： I agree 100%. There are so many that can help us do more of that and explore the world, do things with other people in the background, figure out what makes sense for you. And I'd argue even the "get things done" has a soul — but a very different soul. It's not trying to replace any human relationships. None of this really is — it's all trying to augment relationships. It's not trying to make you perceive it in some sort of human way. It's trying to really be a tool. That's what we are as technologists — we're tool makers. We make things, and they can be net neutral, and it's our job to infuse humanity into them.

主持人： I love that. It does feel like this is a technology to explore our humanity — and we've never had that before. We've had a lot of technology to extend our intellect. That's let us do incredible things — SpaceX this week is the best example. But we really haven't had any technology that's helped us be a little more self-reflective. Social products are fascinating, but exploring us as groups instead of us as individuals.

Josh： That's a really profound way to think about what we can do with this new technology. So much of it is helping you be better. I've seen these great little apps helping people learn to date by practicing first-date conversations. It sounds funny — we're both older and not in that lifestyle right now — but if you're nervous about something, being able to practice is incredible. I haven't done one of these in a long time. I have to admit, I did use a little bit of going back and forth to ChatGPT to just practice talking and getting back into this mindset. That's how these things help us be better.

Gen Alpha：最强的社交一代？

主持人： It would just be so funny and ironic if the result of all this technology is that Gen Alpha — they're just the most incredible social apex predators. The art of conversation is back. It's like the 1950s.

Josh： That would be amazing. We always forget — but that's the generation they've grown up with. They've grown up fully online, fully mobile. We've seen some downsides. A lot of things are happening right now to even prevent social media for people under certain ages — mostly because we haven't all done the work to make these as safe and additive as possible. On the other hand, this generation is going to know how to adapt and get the most out of this technology, hopefully in better service of others. Hopefully look at examples of what's gone right in the world that they can live up to — and what's gone wrong that they can say it's our turn to go fix.

主持人： It's fascinating — this class graduating right now is the first class that grew up, had ChatGPT for all of college. What I think ChatGPT or broad AI lets you do is what they call "the greats" in college — studying ancient Roman, ancient Greek, everything related to it, an individualistic very abstract intellectual exploration of things just for the sake of it. That's a lot of what the technology lets you do. So I hope they are as enthusiastic about it as we are.

Josh： I really hope so. One thing that's really heartwarming for me when I talk to kids is when they tell me, "Hey, I don't use it to do my work because honestly I just know that's not what I'm going to get graded on — I might get caught or something else. But what I use it for is to help me make my work better." The fact that you can turn to ChatGPT when you've done some writing and go, "How do I improve this?" — that's such a powerful tool to have in your arsenal.

I've been using new Siri on my Mac — you write something and they go, "How's this sound? How could I spice this up? How could I get my point across better?" The tips really help you go, "okay, that's a really good tip — I'm going to put that into what I'm writing." When you use it that way, these tools are very additive as opposed to replacement.

Eugenia 的洞见：花时间 vs. 省时间

主持人： Somebody we both know, Eugenia — you might have introduced me to her, or at least whispered a good word on my behalf. Thank you for that. We're so proud to work with her. She said this thing that was so profound: look, people are not trying to save time. They're trying to spend time.

Josh： I love that.

主持人： The entire original sin mistake we're all making in assessing these companies is that the average consumer doesn't know what to do with the blinking cursor. They actually need to have things pushed to them so they can explore, and not have to be so high agency.

Josh： This idea of "time well spent" is exactly the right method of what consumers want. This is why building for consumers is so different than building for enterprises — you can't just start me with something and "I've got a problem, I'll dig into it, give me a blank cursor."

What are the ways you want to best spend time? There can always be a lowest common denominator — more scrolling, more anything, just keep going infinitely, just keep pushing this button in this game and see what happens. You can pull people and lull people into that. But the best products can be smarter and actually take us from idling away time to spending it better.

You want to say to an AI, "Hey, I'm a little bored," or "I need some ideas of what to do" — and have it spark you, give you motivation, encourage you to go do things. Whether it's get out in the world, or do something new, or think about something, or be entertained — it doesn't always have to be work or learning. It can just be fun, but still things that add value, where you reflect afterwards and you're like, "I'm really glad I did that."

创业方向请求

主持人： Maybe to bring us home — give us your kind of request for startups. What are the areas you're personally most interested in? What's underexplored?

Josh： Coming out of having worked inside Apple for a long time, and at Robinhood, and coming back to venture — I'm thinking a lot about what is this next wave of all the ways we connect as people, spend time well, whether that helps us travel better, manage our finances better, manage our health better, get things done, learn how to do our how-tos, get through that list. All these ways we can use this new technology to help us get through our lives, spend it really well, and connect with other people.

Pretty open-ended — because so much right now is built for productivity and enterprise: "I can make this team more effective, here's 20 workflows, here's 30 prompts that help everybody." I'm like, what are the things that make me so happy to go, "I'm using this every day and you should too — and I can't wait till we all are, because it makes it better for everyone."

Josh 在 a16z 的角色

主持人： Take us through what you're going to work on here at a16z.

Josh： I am so excited to be back in the startup world and get this opportunity to help Andreessen Horowitz portfolio companies figure out their way through this consumer landscape — figure out how to grow, tell their stories, build their products. And then I'm really excited to help all the future Andreessen Horowitz portfolio companies — let's figure out who they are, what they're doing, how I can help them, how we can help them figure out their paths. And when it's the right time, make sure they come in and we as a team really fund them.

主持人： Talk about, for a moment, what are the specific challenges you've worked through with companies? Is it about distribution? About M&A? What are some of the ways?

Josh： The stuff I love helping with most is thinking about the product and the product loop. How do you onboard people to get to that retained experience? What do you need to do to get people all the way up the ladder? How do you let them bring other people into that network? What does a core product do for them every day? And how do you evolve a single-use product into multiple uses and scale that as a product platform?

So helping people right in the heart of what that product and product strategy is — but also happy to help talk about how you organize your product team and growth team. It's completely changed in this new era. Things are more different for a product management function and an engineering function in the past six to seven months than the past 30 years of my career. It's incredible how much has changed — and navigating that, knowing what worked in the past but translating that to modern times.

Then obviously helping companies figure out their own path, whether that is M&A. There was a period earlier this year where I had a couple different friends going through M&A processes — I was talking to them multiple times a week, helping them think through the options, how to set up right for every meeting.

And lastly is how to tell your story. The one thing I've gotten this incredible masterclass on, getting to work at Apple, has been right in the heart of: how do we tell a compelling story to regular people? How do we take this world of AI and intelligence and distill it into products that people would want to use and bring into their lives every day? I'm super proud of what the team put together at this most recent WWDC and how we've told the story of Apple Intelligence and Siri AI. I can't wait to bring more of those lessons to companies as well.

结语

主持人： Incredible. How can people get a hold of you?

Josh： They'll be able to email me at jelman@a16z.com, find me through all the social medias, find me through a friend. I'm really excited to help.

主持人： I love it. Josh, welcome to the team.

Josh： Thank you. Excited to be here.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：苹果的"无魂设计"恰恰是它的护城河
Josh 在谈到新 Siri 时说了一句容易被忽略的话——"它不需要奉承你，不需要说'你今天看起来真棒'"。这看似平淡，实则是对整个 AI 消费市场的一次精准切割。
当全行业（ChatGPT、Character.ai、Replika）都在争夺"陪伴"和"情感共鸣"这块高地时，苹果反其道而行之，把 Siri 定义为**"有灵魂但不假装是人"的工具**。这是一个极其昂贵的战略选择——它主动放弃了最容易产生留存和 DAU 的情感粘性维度，转而押注"工具性人格"。
这里藏着一个跨领域的连接：这非常像**日本的"職人精神"对比美国硅谷的"产品人格化"**。日本匠人做的茶碗不会试图跟你"对话"，但它在你手中的每一次温度反馈都在和你交流。苹果在 AI 时代复活的是这种"道具的克制"——而这恰恰是大多数 AI 创业公司学不来的，因为它们必须靠拟人化来制造留存。
## 二、被掩盖的悖论：Robin Hood 的"彩票化推荐"揭示了金融产品的游戏化本质
Josh 详细拆解了 Robin Hood 推荐机制的关键——**给的不是 10

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] Investing a $120 Billion Balance Sheet with No Outside Investors
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：保险公司是"反预测"的赌徒

大多数人以为保险业的核心是"精算未来"——用概率模型预测什么时候会出事。但 Vlad 抛出了一句颠覆性的话："我们不预测未来，我们为未来的所有可能性做准备"。这不是文字游戏，而是哲学层级的差异。

预测者押注于某个单点，所以一旦错了就崩盘；准备者押注于整个分布，所以无论发生什么都能存活。这其实和达里奥的"全天候"、塔勒布的"反脆弱"是同一种生物。但更隐蔽的洞察是：当一家公司放弃预测，它反而获得了最强的进攻能力——因为它不需要等待"对"的时机，而是任何时机都能下场。Liberty Mutual 的 1200 亿不是在等风口，而是把自己变成了"风口的反向收割机"。

二、跨领域连接：保险浮存金 ≈ Y Combinator 的批次结构 ≈ 大学的终身教职

你乍看之下，保险、风投、学术毫不相干。但它们共享一个稀缺资源：结构性免于即时反馈的资本/时间。

  • 巴菲特的浮存金 = 不必每季度向 LP 汇报的钱
  • YC 的批次制度 = 让创业者在 3 个月内只对一群同伴负责，而不对市场负责
  • 终身教职 = 让学者不必每年发顶刊也能存活

Vlad 透露了一个更深的机制："透明度换取自主权——没有透明度，就没有自主权"。这条法则在三个领域里都成立。永续资本不是"自由"，它是用持续的透明度赎买来的耐心。这反过来揭示了一个让人不安的真相：许多对冲基金宣称的"长期主义"其实是赎买不起的耐心，他们没有付透明度的钱，所以也没有真正的自主权。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：可乐颂悖论——稀缺塑造灵魂，丰裕塑造想象力

Vlad 用 6 岁时在苏联排队买面包的故事，和纽约联合广场一千种可颂的对比，表达的不只是"市场经济好"。更深的洞察在于：稀缺不仅约束你能买什么，更约束你能想象什么。在苏联，"面包就是面包"——这不是物质问题，是认知问题。当一个人没见过可颂的一千种形态，他甚至无法形成"还可以再迭代"的念头。

把这个思维迁移到投资上，就出现了一个惊人的镜像：Vlad 投资框架里最强的部分（"先问要什么 exposure，再问怎么获取"），本质上是在给自己制造可颂式的丰裕。绝大多数 LP 只有一种"工具"（基金投资），就像苏联人只有一种"面包"。当工具集贫瘠，连"我应该想要什么"都被工具反向定义了。这是组织设计层面的"croissant economics"——你的工具集决定了你能想象的投资宇宙。

四、彩蛋洞察：AI 让"折现模型"失效，市场尚未消化

最被低估的一句话是 Vlad 谈估值的部分——"未来变得不可见时，所有倍数是否都应该下调？"。这不是宏观问题，是认识论问题。

历史上的估值下调要么因为利率、要么因为衰退——都是"环境变量"。但 AI 引入的是结构性的存活率不确定：你的现金牛 10 年后可能根本不存在。这意味着——这是真正的盲点——长久期信用利差应该结构性走陡，但今天的信用市场几乎没有定价这件事。Vlad 在很温和地暗示一个万亿级的错位机会：30 年期的 Salesforce 债券，定价像在 2015 年，但风险像在 2030 年。

📝 转录文本

Investing a $120 Billion Balance Sheet with No Outside Investors

开场：保险业的独特位置

Insurance is one of these industries that serves so many different purposes. Where we sit in the economy is quite a unique place. We're not in the business of predicting the future. We're in the business being prepared for all its eventualities.

The market's changing. The world's changing. We need to have the liquidity to always react. We need to be flexible, but at the same time have permanence in stay of some of these businesses because that's what makes you a good partner.

You will likely have trillion dollar companies in 2030 that currently don't exist. And you probably have trillion dollar companies or many hundred billion dollar companies that will not exist. We're starting to see that, right?

一、平台介绍：1200 亿美元的独特结构

Vlad, this is going to be a fascinating conversation given that you sit in one of the most interesting investment seats probably in the world. I think to give people context, the right place to start is for you just to describe the platform — how much money you manage, how it's managed, why that platform is unique and different. The seat itself is so interesting to me, and then we'll go into all the things that you've learned sitting in the seat and building the platform. But first, just ground us in: what is the thing? How big is it? How does it work?

What is the thing? The thing is the balance sheet of one of the largest insurance companies in the world — one of the most diversified insurance companies in the world, Liberty Mutual Group, which has, I would say, two primary insurance businesses that ultimately feed the investment platform.

First is the one that probably most are familiar with — the Liberty Jingle — which is our personalized business, one of the largest in the US. That, of course, is the home and auto component of our business.

And then there is the business that is much more global in nature and really serves a sophisticated set of companies, brokers, partners providing commercial and specialty insurance across many domains of business.

Those two insurance businesses ultimately feed an investment platform that takes the reserves as well as the surplus capital of Liberty Mutual Group, and invests for the benefit of our balance sheet, ultimately for the benefit of our policyholders, so that we can ensure that our promises are always met and have the financial strength behind them.

So we are about $120 billion in capital. And what's interesting about our platform is that it allows us an incredibly unique way of behaving as investors — which is to say we are focused not on any form of third-party capital, which has lots of benefits but also lots of challenges when you manage money. And it allows us to think about investing from a long-term perspective, and it allows us to do the right thing, not the expedient thing, and it allows us to maintain what I would describe as investment hygiene. That is one of the most difficult things to do when you're managing other people's money.

The other part that's really quite unique is the ecosystem that we're in, which allows us to grow our capital base in the service of our policyholders. We are not driven by shareholders whose priority is return on capital in the form of dividends and buybacks. That's not part of our structure, and it allows us to again think about making decisions that are the right decisions, not expedient decisions.

二、保险浮存金的力量：与巴菲特模式的对话

So maybe explain one level more why the insurance idea is so powerful. Very famously, Buffett built a big chunk of Berkshire's success on this idea that if you have a sort of insurance part of your business, you control this float — he would call it float — and that this magic access to capital creates this permanence that allows you to do things others can't do. Maybe build that bridge between the Buffett way of thinking and how it feels to actually operate this thing.

What really is interesting to me is how insurance is one of these industries that serves so many different purposes. The way I like to think about it: one side of our business — and one side of our balance sheet — is all about protecting and syndicating that risk, allowing risk to be taken by the people in the world.

And so we have a very appropriate way we think about that at Liberty. We say we want people and businesses to embrace today and confidently pursue tomorrow. That's what the insurance part of our business does.

When we sell those promises, when we take in those premiums, we then move them to the other side of the balance sheet and we do something else that's really interesting for the economy and society. We invest that float, as Buffett would say — but ultimately invest our policyholders' premiums in order to grow the economy, support the economy, invest in critical infrastructure, fund entrepreneurs, create jobs.

Where we sit in the economy is quite a unique place. We do things on both sides of our business that allow us to protect and create the foundation of commerce, and at the same time grow the economy. That's a unique spot.

三、1200 亿的拆解：储备金、成长信用、成长股权

So coming back to this unique combo — you're providing the value through insurance, hedging people's risk one way to think about it — that creates this pool of capital, $120 billion, that you can then (at least some portion of it, we'll get into that) invest and support the growth of the economy, creation of jobs.

Break down the 120 — how much of that is tightly controlled and has to be a certain way because it's heavily regulated. How much of that is more open? And then for the open portion, how do you think about how to allocate it? It's a lot of money. It's one of the bigger investment platforms in the US. So it's a challenge. That's a lot of money to put out. Something that's going to move the needle for you needs to be pretty big. I'm curious how you think about that.

So 120 is a snapshot. I think that number will be bigger next time we talk a couple years from now. But let's talk about the 120.

I would say you could probably think of it as roughly 70 to 75 billion of that is reserves. So that could be described as tightly managed. Again going back to this notion — of course you want to make sure that whatever happens in the investment portfolio you will always be in position to fulfill that sacred promise of writing an insurance policy to your policyholder.

But I would say even there, I think we have quite a unique approach. We are not just buying investment grade bonds, putting them in the drawer, and waiting for that coupon to come once a quarter and then ultimately maturity. That could be a sleepy, boring way — frankly the way this type of capital pool was managed historically. We're quite innovative. We do lots of different things that allow us to be a liquidity provider into that marketplace. But that's about $75 billion.

I would say you could think about the rest as broken out between what we describe as growth credit and growth equity. Those two pools of capital are growing as a function of our surplus. And to your point, they are a way we can really be a full-service kind of capital provider into all parts of the economy.

Growth Credit

What we've done with our credit business is taken an approach — not of public-private, which currently of course gets lots of headlines — but really what is our levered corporate credit business, and we've put those parts together. So our public credit, high yield, and leveraged loans business sits with our capital solutions business, sits with our direct lending business, and with our partnership structure that's focused on credit. All of them sit together, have one platform, one reporting structure, because we believe the expertise frankly is what's important.

And you'll do direct deals, you'll do manager allocations, you'll do big partnerships — whatever?

100%. The way I like to describe that: people very often start with a product — direct lending, or high yield public, or whatever. I think we asked the question: what exposure do we want in the totality of our business? When you ask that question, the next question is: assuming you could figure it out and have the ability to build that portfolio of risks, portfolio of exposures — what's the best way for me to get those risks?

And the options are many. The challenge is most organizations don't have options. You can take perhaps an organization that really has one way, which is to be an LP. So then you are going out meeting managers and ultimately allocating capital. You have people who are direct originators of that risk and pursue that — perhaps that's a GP — and many more.

Our toolkit is vast. So once we determine what exposure we want, we've got lots of different ways of getting that exposure. And this is really critical because as an investor that sits in our platform, you have the choice set that very few investors have. Do I want that in direct form? Do I want that as a co-invest? Do I want that in some kind of club format with other sophisticated investors? Do I want to be an LP, because frankly the particular risk is so difficult to access, so specialized, that I have no aspiration or ambition of trying to replicate that. I view that as an extension of my workforce, and that's the way I'm going to get that exposure.

Growth Equity

Same applies in our growth equity portfolio. Multiple businesses housed there. One is private equity, second is real estate, third is energy and infrastructure, and fourth what we describe as alternative credit. I just talked about the corporate credit business. Alternative credit tends to be all forms of asset-backed finance, where you're lending not against corporate balance sheets but against ultimately some pool of collateral.

So same concept applies — we've got many different ways we can go to market, get that exposure, and that totality of a toolkit has led to a very, very broad and interesting ecosystem in itself. We get to talk to lots of different people with very interesting approaches to the marketplace, and our job is to be competent across all those choices, because if we are, we naturally become a hub — a hub of both interesting transactions that come our way, opportunities to participate in really unique off-market things.

[广告：Ramp / WorkOS / Felix by Rogo]

Ramp is the only platform built to make your finance team leaner, faster and better, saving businesses 5% annually on average so you can stay focused on growth. Ramp customers grew revenue 3.2 times faster than the average American business. Visa, Vercel, Cursor, Stripe, Notion, 11 Labs, Shopify, and 70,000 other businesses all run on Ramp. Mine does too, and so should yours. Learn more at ramp.com/invest.

OpenAI, Cursor, Anthropic, Perplexity and Vercel all have something in common. They all use WorkOS. To achieve enterprise adoption at scale, you have to deliver on core capabilities like SSO, SCIM, RBAC, and audit logs. That's where WorkOS comes in. Instead of spending months building these mission-critical capabilities yourself, you can just use WorkOS APIs to gain all of them on day zero. Visit workos.com to get started.

Felix by Rogo is a personal finance agent that turns a single prompt into finished client-ready work using your firm's own templates, context, and standards. Send Felix an email like "Take these comments and turn them for me," or "update my tracker with the context of these emails," or "run the ability to pay math on this buyer," and Felix sends back finished PowerPoint decks, Excel models, and sourced research. Learn more at rogo.ai/felix.

四、"House View"：不预测未来，准备所有可能

So especially as you think about the risk portion of this total pie — you mentioned earlier you sort of start with where you want exposures and then you fill the exposures. How do you do that? Because one way to think about this is that you're just a giant asset manager and you've got all these different ways you can express yourselves, but it starts with — I don't know what to call it — a house view. How does the house view get developed and how often does it change?

Let me first tell you what it's not. It is not an attempt in any way, shape, or form to predict the future. In fact, one of the sayings we have at Liberty Mutual Investments is: we're not in the business of predicting the future. We're in the business of being prepared for all its eventualities.

I've been a macro trader. I just think that game hardly works and certainly doesn't work for an institution like ours. God bless those that keep playing it and those that are successful at it.

Our house view is much more about what long-term businesses and franchises do we want to be in. And by the way, the notion of being in a business like private equity — that's not a one or two or three or five year business — has very little relevance of what our feelings may be on the environment in the next two or three years.

What I want to always be in position to do is be valuable to our partners, deploy capital into interesting opportunities, structure our risks in a way that is always cognizant of our obligations to Liberty Mutual Group. Those, by the way, are not only through the lens of making sure we meet those policies — that's sacrosanct — but Liberty Mutual Group is a large enterprise that can decide to add businesses to its structure. It can acquire, and we need to be in position to always have the right balance of liquidity versus long-term investments. So I'd say liquidity management is actually an incredibly important component of how that broader portfolio gets constructed.

We know that credit is going to be a large component of our business and we want to make sure anything we enter, we have the right level of expertise. For example, things we have not really done: we have not expanded much into the European market, largely because we don't think we have the right relationships in place there, the right expertise. Even though that region is more interesting than it has been in the past given all the geopolitical dynamics, it is not a place where we are spending our time. Despite the size of the book, despite the growth, we continue to identify opportunities in the US that we feel a lot more comfortable with.

It's a multi-dimensional view that gets both developed but constantly refreshed at the top of the house for sensibility. There isn't a notion of "we have to be this and we have to be that." The market's changing, the world's changing. We need to have the liquidity to always react. We need to be flexible, but at the same time have permanence in some of these businesses because that's what makes you a good partner and a good investor.

五、什么样的原创者能吸引你？

Let's put the hat of the originator on — whether that's a GP, or someone that has a specific deal, someone that needs capital and has an idea. And they come to you because you've got the reputation you do for being one of these both big, stable, and flexible partners that likes kind of novel stuff. What are the attributes that get your and your team's attention?

Above all, if you're coming from a place of a newish idea and you've got ideas and you're looking for capital partners — one is, we always want to get that phone call. By virtue of what I've described before, our approach to the way we partner with people, I would say these kinds of things come as referrals rather than a barrage of cold calls. And that in itself is super helpful.

What are we interested in? Well, there's a very wide waterfront of things that we do, and we already know that if something falls outside the waterfront, there's obviously going to be a higher burden of proof on that. We may not have the expertise.

What is more interesting: what's the unique proposition being brought forward? Those are not frequent. It's not easy to be original in many of these industries and sub-industries. But we're willing to back people. We're willing to take a risk on people we believe in. If we see integrity, if we see an idea that makes sense, and if we can find a true partnership that has the ability to serve both the originator and Liberty for the long run.

That links to something very interesting that's been very important to me in my time in this role — which is to say: what possesses a professional sitting at a stable large insurance asset manager to take entrepreneurial risk? That's a cultural dynamic and one that shouldn't be taken for granted, because the easy answer is "this falls outside my area of comfort, why in the world would I take the risk?"

We have been incredibly purposeful in developing a culture where people have the incentive — are the types of people that would be curious and interested — and have the governance structure in place to actually take those risks. I view that component of the organization as one of the largest and most important responsibilities I have. Because the first time someone turns that call away or behaves in a manner which doesn't demonstrate curiosity and entrepreneurial spirit, those referrals I talked about earlier — they'll dry up. The reputation is built on that entrepreneurial spirit.

六、为什么不只是买债券？为什么承担风险？

Why take the risk at all? If I think about the $120 billion — like you said, there's a long history of insurance being pretty sleepy, investing in bonds, earning a small spread on the float, and no one gets fired, nothing goes wrong, you can still be a great insurance provider. Why bother at all applying your craft and your career in this space versus a more traditional asset manager? Why is the juice worth the squeeze?

I really do believe what I described before — which is really unique place to sit in the financial system, in the fabric of the economy. But specifically: why not just invest the whole thing in a bond portfolio and go away?

That would prevent you from ultimately being a balance sheet that can adopt new technologies — because that's a constant in our world. It is a balance sheet that is able to adapt to the evolution of the economy. Risks are evolving all the time. The risks that insurance companies took on 25 years ago are very different than they are today, and for sure they will be different tomorrow.

Let me give you an example: data centers. This is a totally different scale of asset and value than has existed before. Insurance balance sheets aren't large enough to just absorb that. That's why you have an extension into all forms of third-party capital coming in. But that's an example of where, if you build a fortress balance sheet, you're able to do things that others will not.

How do you build that fortress balance sheet? Through two main engines of profitability. One is the underwriting part, but that's a thin-margin business. And then through the asset side — where if you simply take the approach of "let me buy a 4% investment grade bond or 5% investment grade bond" versus trying to achieve a 7, 8, 10% return on the totality of your portfolio, it's all the difference in the world. It is a competitive business where the amount of capital you have will dictate the opportunity set that is available to you, both on the liability side and the asset side.

But in a mutual — I'm especially interested, in a mutual structure where there aren't shareholders — I would totally understand it if there was. Like, I was an equity shareholder, you can invest this at a high rate of return, that's good for me. Maybe close the loop for why that's good for the holistic thing.

I would actually reverse that. I think as a public insurer, you're not likely to be able to pursue what we're doing. Because if you're a shareholder of a public insurer, you can bifurcate these two things and say: you have historically not been a sophisticated investor, you've been much more conservative. So what I ask of you, management, is to deliver me a very consistent margin on the underwriting, and I'd like to get as much capital back from you in the form of dividends or buybacks. I don't need you to recreate an investment firm on the asset side of your balance sheet — because if I wanted you to do that, instead I could just take that and I could do it. That's a classic example of conglomerates.

So I think the public sphere therefore operates differently and is held to a very tight standard on the underwriting side. Now the flip side — if you're a mutual, you don't have the forcing function of shareholders to ensure that you are operating at your best. But that's an optional feature of mutuality, that's not a requirement. The only requirement is that you can't raise equity. So I think we've made the choice that we want to be an exceptional operator — and that includes exceptional underwriting results and exceptional investment organization.

How it benefits our policyholders? Well, first and foremost, we are going to be there through thick and thin when inevitably trouble strikes. The other component: our insurance businesses are incredibly diverse. If you think about other large insurers — take a Progressive, incredibly successful company, lots to admire — but they are very focused on a particular vertical: motor in the US. They're incredible at it. Those risks require certain types of balance sheets — they're not particularly long-tailed.

If you think about our mix, we have risks that can come back from 20, 30 years ago and be very fat-tailed. So it is really important to differentiate the balance sheet that a company like Progressive needs versus a company like Liberty Mutual's. Our tails are fatter, our balance sheet requirements are very different.

That goes back to this flywheel: if we do well for our policyholders in terms of underwriting — underwriting efficiently, creating various products that tailor and suit their risks — and then invest our capital well, we can perpetuate the strength in the service of our policyholders.

So if I were to sum that up, the success on the investment side unlocks product or service quality for policyholders — product breadth, future risks that are currently maybe not visible to you but are going to evolve, we can be your partner in risks that you can underwrite that maybe others couldn't because of the nature of the balance sheet.

Correct.

The Berkshire Comparison

Let's say at the most extreme, you could think of Berkshire as being that. People think about Berkshire's insurance operations — the most visible obviously is Geico, the original — but they are the insurer of last resort very often. The reason they can do that is because they've got this incredible balance sheet.

Again, I think Berkshire is in a universe of its own, particularly in the way shareholders have regarded Berkshire — not asking for capital back. But that's an extreme example of what you can do when you have a balance sheet like that.

I had lunch with Ajit Jain one time — the savant that has run Berkshire's insurance business forever. He described what he did when you really dig in: he literally said "I wait around and sit, wait for the phone to ring." And people call me with the craziest propositions, the craziest risks that I can price and underwrite. It sounded much more like Warren's job of waiting for fat pitches. It's sort of the extreme version of this — not the programmatic auto insurance, but wacky stuff that nobody else in the world could do.

So it sounds like part of what you've built and are building is something that moves more out in that direction, and isn't just this rote, repetitive single kind of underwriting.

Exactly. We are incredibly diversified in our insurance businesses. We think about the similarity between not all our insurance lines, but particularly those more esoteric ones — in the commercial and specialty space — as very similar in spirit to the practice of what investing is, which is deploying capital into uncertainty to achieve a return. That's true across both. And the risks tend to be not 6-month or one-year risks — they tend to be multi-year risks. So the same disciplines and the same conceptual framework apply. How do you manage your reserves? How do you manage your liquidity? These concepts go back and forth between the balance sheet.

Let's be clear, these businesses operate in their own spheres and ecosystem. Not a lot of operational synergy, but definitely strategic synergy.

七、个人故事：从摩尔多瓦到美国

One of the things you and I have talked about many times that I think is so important for the context that you bring to the job is the power of America and the American system. I have found this phenomenon that the people that love America most often weren't born here. They're immigrants. They saw some other system and they came to this one. You have an incredible story in this regard. Can you tell that early life story in whatever vivid detail you're able to?

Yes, I do love America. We're going to celebrate America's 250th birthday this summer. When you're born outside the United States, you are exposed to a way of life that is very difficult to actually understand for those that are fortunate enough to be born in the United States. You take certain things for granted that are just — you assume they are like gravity because they just exist, but they're not.

So I was born in Moldova, a former republic of the Soviet Union, currently an independent country right outside of Ukraine. I was very fortunate that my parents decided to uproot their life in 1990 and make the journey to the United States. To be clear, this wasn't a difficult decision — this was something that people could only dream of. We were very fortunate for a number of different reasons to take the path that we did, which was a direct path to the United States.

What is true in the US — always has been true, still true today, despite all the many ways you could criticize America — is that the level of agency you have as a citizen or resident of the US is unparalleled to anywhere else in the world. It is a vast country with vast amount of regional differences, cultural differences. We have done an incredible job of ultimately integrating people into our society. And if you have talents, if you have motivation, there's an infinite amount of ways you can both define what success is, define how you will contribute, and ultimately live a life where you have the option to thrive.

Not everyone thrives, but you have the option to. And that option is not available to the vast majority of humanity, because you'll be burdened by your family's history, your ethnicity, your religion, your government's oppressive system, an inability to move up the socioeconomic ladder because of the way the economy is set up. All those things — and versions or combinations of — are present just about everywhere. But in the US, you have an opportunity.

The Croissant Story

One of the ways I described the fascinating thing about America is — believe it or not — through the lens of a croissant. I remember as a kid in the Soviet Union, about 6 years old or so, my mom would send me to go get bread. The way you get bread is — there's a bread store, and there is really one, maybe two types of bread. There'd be lines outside of it and you'd get the bread. Never went hungry, so I don't want to create that impression. But the view was very simple: bread is bread. Why would you need more bread? You get your loaf and you go have your calories.

In the US, we take the exact opposite view. If you want to reinvent the croissant — which exists in a thousand different ways right around Union Square — you can do that. And if you can figure out a way to make it special to you and to your customer, there will be a market for that.

What is the act of that? That is human creativity. That is humans iterating and perfecting and continuing to apply themselves and to express themselves on something that doesn't necessarily need a different way of consuming calories — but it's beautiful. It's what drives, I think, people in general in the United States: to constantly make little tweaks that make things better, and we all benefit from those.

Life in the Soviet Union

I'm curious what else the experience was like in that first decade of life. The bread story is very illustrative of the power of a market system and permissionless innovation. But what else — paint a little bit more of a picture of what it was like to spend the formative decade of your early childhood there, and the contrast to your experience in the US.

At the highest level: you're not given permission to dream. You are born to survive. You're born with an attitude and a notion of "I need to navigate these ways of life so that I can survive."

I experienced really difficult persecution — of being Jewish in the Soviet Union.

That looked like what?

That looked like being called out in school. I was a 9-year-old kid and I still have memories of that. My parents experienced that in much more stark ways — as a Jew you were not allowed to pursue certain professions, or there would be hard quotas on how many people would be allowed to be in those professions. You would be assigned where you live. There would be university quotas, and so on.

I didn't experience obviously those things, but I experienced a society where that was normal — and Jews were persecuted, many other types of groups were persecuted. The point is, that was normal behavior. It was overt, explicit persecution, bullying — all those things were part of the social fabric. When you're born into that, you are consumed by this notion of "okay, this is my reality. How do I navigate it and survive?" Never mind iterating on innovation — I'm just trying to survive.

I do, you know, I was a kid. I was a reasonably happy kid. Because when you accept those things as part of your life, you don't dwell on them. You don't think of yourself as a victim. You just accept them as they are and you form the rest of your life around them as constants. I have both kid-happy memories as well as some of those stark moments of greyness — of a society that has no spirit, that has no real art. That's not a statement on the people, that's a statement on the way society's constructed and suppresses those otherwise natural human traits.

When you come to the US, you experience literally the inverse of that — this notion of individualism. It's the opposite of what you would experience in a place like the Soviet Union, but I would argue many other societies as well. Like everything else taken to its excessive corner, you would probably find all sorts of issues with individualism as the way to construct a society — and we wrestle with those in the US. But what it does do is it frees a person to pursue their talents, pursue their interests, pursue a network of friends they want to be associated with, in a way that's just impossible elsewhere.

From Worldview to Investing

How does that all map back onto this activity of investing? How does it affect the culture you want to build, the types of people you want to partner with, the types of deals that you're interested in?

First, I would relate it to this other notion of risk-taking, not assuming and not taking anything for granted, not being entitled to anything. This is a fundamental trait that immigrants share. When you come with nothing, just looking for a life, you don't think of yourself as entitled to anything. And I think that carries no matter how your life in the United States ultimately evolves. No one owes you anything. That permeates the spirit of the way I go through my life.

Now, we talked about entrepreneurship — a culture of "why do this at all?" Well, why not? Why wouldn't you want to make something better? We've tried to have that culture at LMI where we don't say "this is good enough." That's not a good way to live. If you're passionate about your work and you're really interested in what you do and you care about your craft, you're going to continue to iterate because it's what you do. And that leads to better results. It's no different in my mind than that silly act of trying to create a better croissant.

In terms of the investing activity — we look for traits of entrepreneurs, of people who are eager to make the world better through the lens of whatever it is that they're doing. The best investors are obsessed with their craft, not because they're financially driven. We look for people who are incredibly passionate and good at what they do, are able to communicate it, and are clear about what they're trying to accomplish. Because if you can't communicate a brilliant vision, it stays in your head and unfortunately doesn't get realized.

八、组合结构的演化

Going back to the composition of the portfolio that you've built — how much of that ends up being in like a specific company you invested in, versus you persistently backed a GP that you're just a constant investor in, versus a one-off partnership with a GP?

It has evolved through time. Historically, the primary way would have been to back a GP. That's a pretty narrow path. Perfectly good one. But we've been very focused on that same thing I described before: we want to have as many ways we can engage and help our business partners — asking the first question: what's the exposure? And then how do we best get that exposure?

The mix today is dramatically different than it would have been 5 years ago. So that's both expressed in the types of exposure. For example, we used to have a meaningful amount of exposure in natural resources. We have much less today. One could have looked at that and said, well, natural resources is a way to get exposure to energy, or perhaps inflation hedge if you wanted to put that lens on. But the way we were getting it was not serving us well, both because of our capabilities — we just didn't have the capabilities to be operators of some of these energy businesses. The second part was they were quite narrow.

Instead today, one of the verticals we have is energy and infrastructure, which is both a credit and equity business, where we in many cases choose to own certain assets or have ownership in certain assets but not operate them. Capital across the capital stack, providing solutions which allow us sometimes to do things like provide credit but have upside exposure via warrants. Certainly back partners where appropriate in parts of the industry that are quite technical, where we would never seek to reproduce that kind of insight.

That is an example of: do we have diversification in our portfolio? Are we benefiting in the moment from that exposure to energy? Absolutely. Would we have gotten the same level of exposure and benefit from our previous way of expressing that? No.

九、"品牌资本"：你卖给 GP 的是什么？

Do you find yourself selling yourself as a differentiated partner to GPs because of all these ways that you can support them, to try to win more allocation to their funds, up against other partners that they might choose? What's the pitch?

You've used this term and I've used it ever since I heard you say it — branded capital.

What is branded capital? Branded capital could mean: for one reason or another, you're viewed as someone a GP should engage with. That could be many things. If you're a mega fund coming through your fundraising cycle, branded capital could be a large state pension which will always write big check. That's not what we do. That's not what we are.

Our brand is to come and help you build a business. Our brand is to be quick in the way we ingest information, ultimately come back with how we want to or don't want to participate, so that we don't waste your time. We operate much more like a GP in that way, and frankly look to hire people that come from GPs or operators rather than just a traditional LP background.

Of course we compete in a variety of spaces, but our reputation continues to build on the way we show up. Any single one of our people that goes out into the world can do tremendous damage or bring tremendous benefit in the way they engage. Because, as you know, if you do great things with 10 business partners, the next 10 things are going to be easier — because at least a few of those are going to come from that network.

We are a hub of incredibly interesting relationships, and we've tried to make sure that we approach that with all the care and diligence and thoughtfulness. If I can identify a way we can be helpful to two of our partners or three of our partners and not be involved, we're always going to do that. We really do feel that these are valuable relationships — business relationships, friendships — and we're rooting for all our business partners. One way or the other, that's going to help our business in the long term.

On this notion of branded capital, is it fair to say that a goal you have is that you want to be one of those LPs that a GP thinks about — like a Yale, or something — where "okay, if Yale's backed this thing, that says something about it, and that brings other capital"?

Yes, that's exactly right. But I would say we want to be even more bold than that. There's a notion of a name being on the capital roster that allows others to come in — that's an asset you have as that type of LP. But we want to be much more than that. We want to be known for our creativity to structure solutions, our willingness to take risks that some of those institutions with that halo of a brand just aren't set up to do.

In the case of the venture ecosystem, we're not going to try to replicate that outside our organization. That's where you're competing truly with other capital to get on the capital roster. In other places, that's just not the game. The game is: are you creative? Are you quick? Can you take certain risks that others just don't even think about?

十、地缘政治与全球秩序的变化

This environment is so interesting because it feels as though geopolitics and the kind of changing shifting nature of global order and power structures matters to investing outcomes for the first time in a long time. A whole generation of investors that is retiring right now didn't really have to think too much about this. There was kind of relative global peace and stability post-World War II — this Pax Americana — and that's changing. How do you think about that variable?

I love history. So I'm particularly tempted to engage in these things. But a couple of things I try to remind myself of: whatever it is that you are living through, we are living through. It feels particularly acute to us. It always feels like the sharpest moment — probably isn't.

In my career, I've now been through more than enough of various crises. I distinctly remember walking after a long day on Zoom in March or April of 2020. It was a rainy day. It was like a 10, 11 hour marathon on Zoom. Just needed to walk outside, and I'm walking around and thinking: is it really possible that my life happened to coincide with this moment in humanity where a pandemic was going to completely upend society and human life as I know it? I told myself odds are very small. And then I remind myself there were many pandemics before that would wipe out large parts of humanity. Yet humanity goes on.

I have those thoughts when it comes to the unbelievable moment in technological progress that we're living through, that feels really real and very different. You can imagine society 10, 15 years from now will look nothing like what it looks like today. But that was also probably true during the industrial revolution for the people that looked back 15 years after the steam engine became mainstream.

And then I think about the geopolitics today — they do feel like we are certainly breaking the order that had governed economic flow, security architecture across the globe that has been in place more or less since World War II. You can make the argument that along the way we had some really major shifts, like the Berlin Wall falling. But it's been a period where certain norms in international relations and certain alliances held through all that, and it does feel like that's changing.

That in itself, I don't think affects investing much if you're focused in the US. What does clearly matter is that the economic architecture is changing — from an energy perspective, from a supply chain perspective — and there are real investing opportunities and risks that evolve from that.

I continue to think that the US is endowed with inherent advantages — whether that's the ability to innovate, whether that is the energy abundance that we ultimately have. All those things continue to conspire for American exceptionalism. But we've also gotten quite accustomed to a world where just-in-time inventories were a thing, and now that's challenged. The ability to identify the cheapest pockets of labor or competitive advantages — like the economics book would say — that's going to potentially have structural impediments, which has implications on inflation, on rates. How does all that balance with this incredibly deflationary impulse from technology? I'm not sure.

I think we are reasonably good at identifying the variables that drive economic outcomes — but I think we're terrible at assigning weights to them. And so that's why forecasting is next to impossible. You may get the right kind of issues, but you don't know how they interact with each other. So I do think we're living through a moment. I do think it's a reset in the way Pax Americana governed, but I don't think it's a reset away from American power in the world, at least on a relative basis. The world continues to need America.

[广告：Vanta / Ridgeline]

Vanta automates security and compliance for over 16,000 fast-moving companies like Ramp, Cursor, and Harvey, keeping them audit ready around the clock. Get $1,000 off at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is the first end-to-end system of record with embedded AI for investment management firms running portfolio accounting, reconciliation, reporting, trading, and compliance on one unified platform. Request a demo at ridgeline.ai.

十一、AI 与估值的新认识论

The other side of the coin that you mentioned is the changing technology landscape. Arguably that's the bigger one — than very unpredictable geopolitics. This seems more predictable: like 10 years from now there's going to be a lot more stuff that's changed as a result of AI. How does that filter through to your investing? And of course you get very tactical here — you think about software, things like that. But I'm also curious just more holistically: what are the conversations like inside Liberty about this topic?

What's super different about this technology than other versions of how this kind of came about, is it requires people to engage with it, get into a relationship with it, have agency. It's not a software package that the technology department is going to install on your desktop. It's an absolute superpower that's given to you to get the thoughts out of your brain and have the superhuman assistant that is able to rationalize your thoughts, present them in a coherent way, interact with you. It makes you sharper if you go back and forth and jostle with it, and really become an editor as opposed to just taking that first output and saying "oh, good enough." Because that's where slop tends to live. If you just ask for something and you get it back, it will give you generalities and it will drive everything to an average — that's what these models are. In order to get the best out of them, you need to engage with your knowledge, your experience, your ideas and creativity.

In the investing sphere, creativity is such an incredibly important part. The ability to take an obscure — or frankly well-organized — set of data and find insights that are not easily observed from it. That's the art of investing. And now you can iterate on that in such powerful ways. I find myself using AI every single day, more and more so.

And frankly, it raises other questions for me. The more I spend of my day with AI, I'm actually not spending it with my colleagues. So I begin to worry about that. How much are you taking from those messy relationships — that are human relationships and messy ways of getting information — into the super interesting, smart, and efficient way of interacting with artificial intelligence? I don't know how that part is going to play out. Because if you take it to the max, it's isolating.

The Multiples Debate

What are the biggest debates? If I think about the team you've built — you plus the heads of the various parts of the organization — what are you guys debating and discussing most right now in this combined interesting environment: geopolitics, AI, everything else going on?

One of the newer ones that is really interesting is this notion of: how do you think about valuations across — not just software (software has been the most talked about one) — but how do you think about valuation of businesses in a world where the future is increasingly invisible?

It was perhaps always invisible, but you could get comfortable that certain things had a lot more staying power through thick and thin than not. And whenever you have that dynamic, you could put a higher multiple on things. That's how you rationalize paying a price for an asset.

Today, you kind of got to ask: do I really know which businesses will thrive 10 years from now or 15 years from now? I think that's really difficult. And that could be everything from yes, software, but maybe perhaps even Home Depot or John Deere — things that are not obvious in the AI crossfire. So that leads you to a question of: should multiples be actually lower across the board?

I don't think I've experienced a question like that in my career, where you question multiples based on macroeconomic variables. Oh, inflation's higher, rates are higher, therefore multiples should come down — or some version of this — is very different. You're literally saying the future is so unpredictable that how can I possibly place some higher multiple on something. That is a really fascinating question because it comes against the backdrop of possibly very favorable macro, which historically would have just had higher multiples.

Another way to put it is: you will likely have trillion-dollar companies in 2030 that currently don't exist, and you probably have trillion-dollar companies or many hundred-billion-dollar companies that will not exist. And we're starting to see that, right? We're starting to see therefore more volatility. The next extension is: there's a multiple question. Now you also have potentially structurally higher volatility. You combine that with some of the tactical stuff like perhaps the SEC will not require quarterly earnings — so you can make an argument that volatility is just going to be structurally higher going forward.

Credit Implications

The other part — everything I've talked about for now is through the equity lens. In the credit space, same kind of concepts apply. Am I worried about four-year paper in most of the software names? Probably not. They're contracted out. This is not a four-year issue. That paper should be money good.

Would I be worried about holding 30-year credit on Salesforce, or Oracle, or any of these things? I just think that it's a much riskier proposition. So it would feel like that should drive steepness in credit curves. If that's a structural shift that takes place, that will change capital market behavior.

I have not experienced a framework or a time in my career — and I can't really go back through history and identify something similar — where it's not really the macro conditions per se (although you can maybe call this macro) that are driving potential repricing of equities and long-duration credit and volatility, all because it's just uncertain as to how this technology will evolve and change the fabric of the economy.

We've had things that change the fabric of the economy, but it would be slow burn. With Salesforce — this is a company that is embedded in the vast majority of large companies in the United States and maybe globally. The first question was: are people just going to vibe-code their own CRM? No, of course not. I sit in a large enterprise — that's absurd, that's not the question. The question is: that trillion-dollar company I mentioned before, that isn't even around today, that's still an idea somewhere — will they ever use Salesforce as part of their ecosystem? And if the answer is no, that should absolutely be a massive headwind to the valuation of Salesforce. Even though every Fortune 500 company may use Salesforce into perpetuity, it's going to be a slowly — it's a different business. It's a cash cow business, deserves a different multiple. That's what I think the markets are wrestling with in the public sphere, which has its own cascading effects into the private sphere.

十二、公开市场 vs 私募市场

So if we've got this rise and fall of the trillion-dollar — a new Mag 7 is emerging or something like this — the question of public versus private seems really important. The three or four biggest companies in private markets, if they go public this year or next year, will be three of the 10 biggest companies in public markets. That's never happened before. How do you think about this?

I think public markets are substantially more difficult to hold than private markets. Many parallels to this. You don't think about the value of your house every day, even though it changes arguably every day. But if you own a public REIT, you'll probably look at it every day and have some feelings from it.

But fundamentally, equity exposure is equity exposure. So we don't think about moving between the two based on this dynamic. It comes back to: what do we own in private markets?

The more significant reason private markets have evolved the way they have in the past decade is — you went public historically for very specific reasons. You needed to raise a certain amount of capital that was simply unavailable in private markets. They were just not robust enough. And if you wanted to grow your business, you would have to go to the public markets. There was a clear element of prestige with going public. That was a milestone in a company's history.

The trade-offs: you give up a significant amount of control, the ability to make decisions for longer horizons and sit through difficult moments — because public markets punish you, shareholders react, boards react.

Private markets have more or less addressed those challenges. You can now raise gigantic amounts of capital. So the capital need has been solved. The prestige and milestone thing — that's kind of gotten diluted. These companies have grown so large, we all know what they are. And now the cost of being public is actually quite high — whether that is a literal cost of the amount of compliance required, or people being really careful about saying "do I want the kind of pressures that are naturally present in public markets in the way I'm going to run my company?" Can I operate on more of a quarter-to-quarter, year-to-year basis, or is my business really going to suffer if I have to operate that way? I really need that 3- to 5-year window which public markets very rarely give.

So I think that's what's driven the growth of private markets. And some of those things may mean revert. Maybe regulatory burden reverts — that's at least fixable. But the main reason of capital being available to you as a private company, I think that stays. So this balance will persist. If you are an equity investor, you should look at the equity risk first and then decide what is the best way to get it.

We have not stopped by any means investing in the private markets. There are other reasons why our balance sheet specifically is probably not best suited for public market exposure — that is specific to our balance sheet. Doesn't mean we don't participate. Doesn't mean we don't take opportunities when they look particularly compelling. But we will continue to largely focus in the private space for our equity exposure.

十三、高盛文化的留存与卓越的代价

You and your senior team came from Goldman. What cultural crossover happened there? What did you take with you, what did you leave behind? Goldman, especially when you were there, was a very distinctive culture.

Goldman is one of these places where, when you're there, you're amongst such talented and driven people. It's a hard place to be and it's a thrilling place to be. But you're particularly actually appreciated once you're not in it — because of all the things that you didn't know you were learning, that you were in fact learning.

I think we brought a drive for excellence with us. I'd say that's the most overarching theme. I find it implausible to just sit still — to take something and say "this is good enough, why should we do this, this involves risk." It's just this motor that I think people that come and have been reasonably successful in organizations — it's inherent to them that they are always pushing forward, even though another person may look at that and say "why do you bother?"

Why is that enjoyable? Why is excellence fun or enjoyable or rewarding?

I think about this a lot. I don't know. First of all, I clearly don't know the answer to it. But I think as I've gotten older, I think about this in very existential way. When you have a family, when you think about the incredibly important lens that provides — particularly through the lens of your kids — you could split your identity in so many different ways. And if you overswing to one or the other, something suffers. Everybody's got their own equilibrium.

But I've continued to find that I get an incredible amount of personal satisfaction from building and being part of an organization that is making progress, that is making things better. By "things" I mean — that can mean product, that can mean customer experiences, that can mean the careers of the people that are in my vicinity. It's one of the most satisfying things in the world to have you help other people progress through their careers. You don't think about that when you're younger, but it is truly satisfying.

So I've gotten more comfortable through time with spending more of myself at work than I have before. Before, I would be very wary of that because I didn't want to have somehow my kids see less of me because I am a workaholic. But my whole frame around that is — I don't think I'm a workaholic. But I am obsessed with making things better. And being part of this organization and team — it's not about you, it's about the organization as a whole and how it can continue to get better — gives me real satisfaction.

I have three boys, so inevitably I want to set an example for them, best I can through my experience, of what it means to be a productive human being. What it means to be a good father — unbelievably important. What it means to show them love in supporting care. Try to do that best I can every single day. I think they would find that they get a healthy balance, and that balance moves through time.

My 17-year-old, my firstborn, literally needs a lot less of me today than he did 10 years ago. I think that's less of something he thinks about because it's so natural. It's something that I think about and I grapple with. Like, is it okay that we interacted for 10, 15 minutes? It was maybe a high-quality interaction, but it was 10 to 15 minutes. I go to bed thinking about that sometimes. But I also think about — this is a forever thing. You think about your parents a lot less than they think about you. That will forever be true. It's true of your kids. It's true of the way you probably interact with your parents — but your parents probably think about you much more than you think about them. Just reality.

十四、永续资本与务实乐观

I'd love to close asking about two concepts that I think are so powerful that you've alluded to a bunch. One is just permanent capital — what it's actually like. Everyone talks about this as like a nice thing to have, but what is it actually like managing it? Like, you're not permanent. Nobody's permanent. Ideally, if you do a great job, this thing will go far beyond you. So careers are not permanent — they're transitory.

And then the second thing is this notion, kind of related to the question on where you came from and where you grew up, of — I think you call it pragmatic optimism. These two concepts are interesting to me in combination, and I thought it'd be a fun place to have you riff.

The Downside of Permanence

The downside of permanence is that people change.
