这背后藏着一个更深的政治结构问题：在特朗普高压、内部19人分歧极大（8人不加、剩余分散）的情况下，主席选择"不交SEP"+"听市场的"，其实是一种博弈论意义上的甩锅策略——把降息/加息的责任从"我决定"变成"市场逼我"。这跟一个CEO在董事会分裂时说"我们做用户调研决定"是同一种心理动作。

二、跨领域连接：两年期美债，是金融市场里的"前额叶皮层"

视频反复用一张图说"白线（2Y收益率）领先蓝线（联邦基金利率）"。但如果用神经科学的视角看，这其实是一个非常有趣的镜像结构：

  • 联邦基金利率 = 大脑的运动皮层（真正下达动作）
  • 2Y收益率 = 前额叶皮层（提前规划、预判、整合所有信号）
  • CPI/PCE/就业数据 = 感觉输入
  • 美联储Speakers = 基底神经节（决定是否要"否决"前额叶的冲动）

历史上Fed经常"否决"市场预判（2021年最典型——市场前额叶早就喊通胀了，Fed硬说transitory）。但现在Powell宣布"我不否决了"，意味着整个系统的决策回路从'多巴胺-基底节调节'退化成了'纯反射弧'——这会显著放大市场波动率，因为去掉了那个"踩刹车"的中间层。

小淘其实点到了这一层（"放弃沟通会增加波动"），但没说透：未来VIX的中枢可能系统性抬升，因为Fed主动放弃了波动率压制器的角色。这对所有卖波动率策略（covered call ETF、JEPI/JEPQ类）是一个结构性逆风，但视频里完全没提。

三、英特尔减仓决策里藏着的一个"反共识"哲学

小淘说："估值高从来不是清仓的理由，仓位占比才是。" 这句话听起来像常识，但实际上和99%散户的操作逻辑是颠倒的——散户的本能是"涨多了就跑"，而他的逻辑是"涨多了，仓位被动放大了，所以削回原比例"。

这其实是Ray Dalio的风险平价（Risk Parity）思想的家庭散户版，但应用在了个股层面。更深一层的连接是——这种思维模型和进化生物学里的"种群密度自调节"完全同构：当一个物种数量过多时，不是因为它"变坏了"才需要削减，而是因为它挤占了生态位的稳定性。

这里有一个真正反直觉的洞察：小淘对Intel的处理方式，恰恰证明了他不是"价值投资者"，而是"组合工程师"。 他口头说"基本面优先"，但行为上他的最高优先级其实是组合的反脆弱性——基本面是用来筛选标的的，但仓位管理才是真正的α来源。这跟巴菲特"集中持仓不动"的哲学是相反的，更接近Jim Simons的统计套利思路。

四、一个被所有人忽略的隐藏信号：Accenture暴跌的真正含义

视频说Accenture跌20%是因为"booking转负"+"市场担心AI颠覆咨询"。但真正值得警觉的跨领域信号是：

Accenture的现金流贴现率（FCF yield）跑到了银行股之下。这意味着市场在用"瓦解中的传统行业"（比如百货、有线电视）的折现率定价Accenture——而这通常是一个行业从"成长股"被重新归类为"末日股"的转折时刻。

历史镜像：2018年的IBM、2015年的Xerox、2010年的Nokia——它们都曾在某个季度因为"一个领先指标转负"被市场永久重新定价，再也没回到原来的估值中枢。Accenture这次的5.1%股息率，可能不是"便宜的安全边际"，而是市场在给它办葬礼前的最后一份慰问金。

这和芯片股估值28.6倍形成了一个极其讽刺的镜像：同一个AI叙事，把硬件抬到天上，把服务踩进地里。 而历史上，每一次这种"硬件溢价 vs 服务折价"的极端分化，最终都以毛利率向中间均值回归告终——这意味着英伟达的50%净利率和Accenture的6倍现金流，都不是稳态。

📝 转录文本

开场与本期框架

Hello, everyone. Welcome to a professional US stock channel that doesn't just talk about news. I'm Xiao Tao, a market analyst. Welcome to the update on Thursday. Friday is not open. So today's content, you can listen to it without worry. Because no matter what I say, the stock market will not rise or fall tomorrow. I wish you a happy weekend. Happy weekend to all the American viewers.

Today's content, because after yesterday's US-China meeting, I did a live broadcast. The live broadcast time is very long, so I will quickly summarize the first show of Kevin Walsh at the US-China meeting yesterday. Then we will talk about the market today. After the release of the financial report by Accenture, there was a very big drop. Why did it drop? Is there a difference between each shareholder and each shareholder between software blocks? Finally, I will share with you the situation of the chip block.

So Okay, I'll stop talking and start today's content right away. Before we start, I remind you to remember to leave a comment, like and forward.

一、周四市场回顾

Let's first review the trend of asset prices on Thursday. The US dollar and US debt revenue have a very large amount of US dollars. The US debt revenue has seen a small drop, but if we add the trend of the US debt market on Tuesday and Thursday together, the US debt revenue of two years has shown a very large drop.

So from the results, we can conclude that at least the market thinks that the US-China Fed meeting of Kevin Walsh is biased. We must not use the trend of US stocks and the market to understand Kevin Walsh or how to understand the meeting of the US Fed to make a connection. The trend of US stocks, US debt revenue and the US Fed, especially within a short period of time, is not very relevant.

Even if you still remember, in the beginning of 2022, the attitude of US stocks and US bonds, including the attitude of US stocks and the United States, came out of a gap for a while. In the end, US stocks will approach the US bond in this direction.

So the market was doing very well on Thursday. The stock market was at 1.1%, the Nasdaq was at 1.9%, and the Dow was at 0.1%. The main increase in the stock market and the Nasdaq is from the 1st quarter when the major tech stocks rose by 1.5%. The second most important point is that the chip version rose by 6.6%. The chip version also pulled the YTT directly to 112%. So the YTT has risen by 10% so far, and the Nasdaq rose by 14.1%. Most of the increase came from the chip version.

二、美联储会议复盘：Kevin Walsh首秀

Then we will summarize the US-China summit on Wednesday with the first show of Kevin Walsh.

点阵图与SEP

First, on the point-and-correct map, the remaining 18 members of the United States will be suspended once or more than the previous year. Only one person among the remaining eight people will be suspended this year. Only one person among the 19 members of the United States will be suspended this year. The rest of the people are considered to be suspended or suspended.

Kevin Walsh did not submit his prediction. Why? Because he thought that if he had no way to make a prediction, there was no need to submit it.

第一点：决心把通胀压回2%

Then let's take a look at the focus in his speech and Q&A. First of all, he talked about the fact that he was determined to bring the inflation back to 2%. This also expressed the determination of the US Federal Reserve and all other US Federal Reserve members to bring the inflation back to 2%. This sentence itself at least repeated three times.

Although he did not give his own opinion, the SEP did not submit, and then someone asked him what he would do next, he did not give any answer. The market also directly pulled up the two-year US debt rate and there was a rise. The market began to set prices, and the first increase was made in September. In fact, the market actually listened to this sentence. How do you press the balance back by 2% is through the increase. This is the first point.

第二点：世界变化快，政策要快速适应

The second point is that the world is changing very fast now. This means that the monetary policy of the United States and the United States should adapt and adapt quickly. It is actually very optimistic.

In 2021, the entire United States and the United States had said that the inflation was short, but it did not increase in time. It only began to increase in 2022. So the United States and the United States in this area is that if we see a very urgent need to increase, we will increase, but not for some political correctness put the matter of the increase drag on.

So the American Fed of Kevin Walsh this year and before not the same. Before, I and you compare the Fed is the sea inside the navigation of that ship — it is to turn from the decline to the increase is to need a very long time of this transition, and every step of the transition of time we can all be sure. But Kevin Walsh this year, the American Fed is not the same. His speed of this transfer may be very fast. The former US Fed may take at least three months to six months to transfer; Kevin Walsh is very likely to start transferring in just two months. For example, now it's June, July, August, September — the US Fed will be able to reopen again the possibility of increase.

第三点（最大变化）：让市场来解读数据

The third point is also the biggest change I think. Kevin Walsh said that he said let the market decide how to make this feedback to the data.

[过渡段：关于美联储与市场的相互引导关系的长段论述，原文以反复结构呈现。]

Because how the US dollar reads the data will affect the US dollar's monetary policy. And the US dollar's monetary policy is the reason why the asset price, especially the US stock, is rising or falling.

2021 is a very good example. I have mentioned it many times. In 2021, the inflation rate has reached 5%. The US dollar market has already believed that the US dollar will increase. But the bubble has been saying that inflation is short. They are still in the QE, so the US stock price rose all the way up until the beginning of October, when the bubble began to pivot. In December, January 2022, the US stock, especially the big tech side, officially began to fall.

So the turning point of the US-China relationship is the time when the asset price falls. It's not that the bond market starts to price increase, and the asset price must fall. So at a certain time, the US Fed can disagree with the market's view, and disagree with the view of the two-year US debt rate. They have their own idea, which is to pull the US debt rate or the bond market towards their view.

But now Kevin Walsh says, "Don't wait for our interpretation. You can directly look at how the market interprets this data." Because whether it's big financial data or CPI, PCE, the market will immediately make a response. Kevin Walsh's view is that you don't have to think about how we think and how we interpret it. You can just take the market interpretation as a more important judgment.

三、为何这是"致命的转变"

I personally think this is a more fatal reason. Why? It means that the market is right. We listen to the market. It changed. I said before that it changed the thinking model that I shared with you before.

If you listen to the market or take the market's voice as one of the very important reference indicators, the market price will be 90% increased in September. If the market is expected to increase now, we know that Kevin Walsh was a Trump's point of view. Trump wanted to lower the interest rate. If Kevin Walsh takes the market's voice as a very important reference, then is it a raise or maintain the same rate in September?

Another point is that it is irrational in the short term of the market. So the market will fluctuate very much in the short term. If the US-China relationship is often more distant, the US-China relationship must pull the market back. But if it gives up this kind of communication means now, gives up releasing this signal to the market, it wants to become a background version, and let the market be the leader, then it will often increase the fluctuation of the entire market. If the fluctuation of the US-China relationship increases, the fluctuation of the US-China relationship will definitely increase.

四、9月加息的可能性

In the video outside of the week, I talked about it. I said that they were going to start the holiday season as early as September, and I said that it was almost impossible at that time. Because according to the previous United States and the United States, this kind of rhythm, he didn't have time to make a turn.

But on Wednesday, when I finished listening to Kevin Walsh yesterday, I listened to Kevin Walsh this time. The United States and the United States — I said September is very likely because if it follows the market and the market has started to set prices, it is possible that September will really increase.

So now the market price increase in September is at 88.9%, which is nearly 90% likely to increase in September. It actually fits the signal that Kevin Walsh wants to release to the market. He said, I refer to the market and he said that the entire US dollar is now unified to press the inflation back to 2%. He said it three times. Doesn't this represent the current decision of the US dollar?

So this is why I think this time Kevin Walsh is very positive. The bond market also made a relative response. Then the market will expect to increase by 1.5 times throughout 2026. If we don't see the increase in inflation after the end of September, it will not be unexpected to increase by one time in December.

So the US dollar of Kevin Walsh will definitely be different from before. If it is still the bar one US dollar, I will be very confident to tell you that the price of the market is wrong. But now I can't tell you that it's wrong. On the contrary, Kevin Walsh told us to respect the market price. If it's now, you let me judge. Many people ask me if it will increase in September. If you ask me, I think the probability of increasing in September is very high.

五、历史回顾：市场如何"牵引"美联储

In fact, the US-China relationship, the market, and the bond market have always been a process of mutual influence. But from the historical data, we usually think that the market is leading the US-China relationship. Behind this, it doesn't mean that there is no data to support it.

For example, let's look at the picture. The white line is the revenue rate of the two-year US debt. The blue line is the share price of the US-China relationship. Basically, the white line is higher than the blue line when it rises and lower than the blue line when it falls. What does it mean? When in a period of increase, if the market thinks it will increase, the annual income will start to rise. Then we see that after a certain period of time, the blue line, the gap rate of the US dollar, starts to rise, which means that the market takes the US dollar to go.

2015年末

For example, in the end of 2015, we saw the white line rise first, and then the United States and the United States began to add it in a few months later. So this is the first time in the period of increase.

2018年末

Then at the end of 2018, I remember very clearly because I was in the market. I remember very clearly because in December — December 25th Christmas Day, the last few days of the exchange day, the share of stocks fell very much. It's because of the bubble still determined to do it in December. The market was very disliked at that time. You see the end of 2018 white line, the two-year US bond rate has begun to fall.

Then in the middle of 2019, this is very because it was Trump's government. Then Trump let the bubble go down. Then everyone thought that the bubble was not independent because Trump let the bubble go down. Then in 2019 the market fell down in the second quarter of 2017, and then after a while, the blue line, the US dollar started to decline.

So these two are very good examples of the market taking the US dollar to the group. The market tells the US dollar that you should decrease, you should increase, and then a few months later, the US dollar really respects the market's will to do so.

2021年：经典案例

Another time in 2021, this is a very classic example. In 2021, look at the white line. In September 2021, there was a very large amount of this injury. Because the inflation came out at 5% at the time of the year.

Then look at when the US dollar started to increase. The US dollar only started to increase in 2022. So in the middle of that time, you can understand that the market is taking the US dollar to go. But in the middle of 2021, especially in the second half of the year, there are many time nodes. The US dollar is pushback. The Fed said that I think the passage is short. So the market price is really set. The market price of 200 million dollars is set very, very early. But when it really hits the US dollar, it will be at least half a year later.

When did the US stock market fall? The US stock market did not fall when the US dollar started to rise in the first two years. It was when the US dollar really started to pivot, and started to say that I was going to start to increase. I'm going to start to increase again. After this period, the US stock fell. It was in November 2021 when the growth curve fell first. Then in January 2022, the entire stock market began to fall.

2023年SVB事件与误判

Let's look at the future. In February and March of 2023, we saw a drop in the white line. Why? Because of the SVB incident. After the SVB incident, the price of the two-year market price dropped rapidly. It was expected to fall into a downturn because of the over-expansion. What does the breakout mean? It means that the market has to go to the US dollar in advance and tell the US dollar that you are going to reduce interest rates. But the US dollar did not stop the market. It continued to increase interest rates.

Then after a while, the market found that the SVB incident could be solved and did not enter the breakout. The two-year US dollar-to-bill cabbage trade agreement immediately rebounded. Then at the end of 2023, there will be some sounds of a certain decline. The two-year US debt income will drop again, but the US dollar didn't go down at that time.

So these two times are very obvious — a misjudgment of the two-year US debt or the market debt market. Until when? Until March and April of 2024, the two-year US debt income began to fall again. Then in September 2024, the US dollar officially opened the downward trend.

小结：两年期美债作为先行指标

So the price of the two-year US debt income or the market response to the two-year US debt income is very much reflected in the future. The expectation of the currency policy or the outlook on whether it is going to increase or decrease. The trend of the two-year US debt income is not necessarily correct, but it is at least a pioneer.

For example, in March this year, when the World War I began, oil prices began to rise, and the income of the two-year-old American family began to rise. So does it mean that the next United States will be a global market? Not necessarily. But at least now the market has set a 90% probability.

You can see it in three months. And in the next two months, of course, there will be another US-China meeting in the middle. So in the next two to three months, if the US-China does not push back, if there is no data to support inflation, there will be a slowdown in the increase rate in September. It may increase greatly and may be set at 100% at the end. The US-China does not push back, so the market prediction is correct, or the market prediction is wrong.

六、Accenture（ACN）财报暴跌解析

Okay, let's talk about Accenture. Accenture is a company that I really like. I know two or three of the three institutions. There is one institution that I really like, Accenture. But it was sold a while ago. It's basically capitulated. I said that in the first quarter, many institutions have already capitulated in the software section. So the position of the warehouse is basically very low.

But it is not to mention that Accenture has dropped nearly 20% after the release of the report on Thursday. In the past year, the total decline rate has reached 58%. The market value is only 78 billion.

股息率与"软件公司金融化"

Then we look at the dividend rate in the back view mirror — it has come to 5.1%. This 5.1% is mainly because after the whole market value dropped, if it can still pay or cash in so many of these dividends, then this dividend ratio has reached 5.1%.

So now many software companies give me a feeling, because their current valuation is already very low, if you can still keep this dividend, what has this software company become? Become some financial, become some utility, even more than financial than the financial version. Many banks have a lower valuation.

暴跌真因：Booking

Why did the drop appear after the financial report was published? I think the market interpretation is because their index is not enough, but what I see is that it is mainly because of their booking. Because booking is the leading index for future revenue, the drop of 2% in the same rate of increase in booking has appeared, so it is not a positive interpretation for the market.

The story line that the market has been running is that they consult this area of business. The market believes that AI will cause a great deal of damage to the business of consulting. So now for software companies, because each software company has a different business, so they are basically different.

For companies like Accenture, the market writes a story line that consulting will be damaged. So they have to prove that they can't be damaged in consulting. And their increase is accelerating. Otherwise, the market will basically have a hard time for a basic return or a return on value. At the same time, if there is a negative growth in booking, it will basically collapse for the market.

估值视角：P/CF 与 P/E

After the fall, let's look at P/E. Actually, I don't look at P/E. I look at P/E. Why do I look at P/E here? Because their cash flow is still so high. Their valuation is down again. P/E, if you look at the back of the world, is 6 times. What is 6 times? If you can still maintain such a cash flow now, you can buy the entire company in 6 years. It's almost like that.

And if you look at PE, in the next three years, because this company, Accenture, is a low-income company, its revenue is very low. The low-income revenue is high. So its EPS is in the high-income range. So 7%, 8%, and 9%.

Similarly, if you look at the current current price to cash flow, 6.36 times in 2008, and 5.82 times in 2009. Indeed, now basically all software companies have a very low valuation, including Adobe, which I own. I think software companies can still run out of companies in the end, so they must look at different software companies differently.

So every software company must be very confident in its basic surface or at least have a certain sense of control, and then you can take part of it or the corresponding warehouse to try. Otherwise, if it's just because, for example, the valuation is very low and then you go to buy, I think you will be very uncomfortable because there is nothing to support you. It's just a valuation, then its valuation can remain low for a long time.

七、芯片板块估值与英特尔减仓思路

Finally, let's talk about the valuation of the chip. I am looking at SOX here. The valuation of the chip is 28.6 times higher than the closing price of JOS. It is close to 29 times the valuation in June. I drew a line here that is 30 times higher. So if it rises to 30 times, it is basically more than 30 times higher than it was in 2024. If it goes to 30 times this time, it is the new high of more than a year ago.

英特尔减仓的逻辑

I also shared with you in yesterday's live broadcast because I talked about Intel as my first-generation warehouse. After the decommissioning, it is still my first-generation warehouse. If it goes to more than 30 times, I should take the initiative to decommission the warehouse on the side of Intel.

The reason for the decommissioning is not that I don't like this company, but the scale of this company's overall investment group is too large. Basically, I still look at Intel as a company, and I don't think Intel's valuation is in a very high position. I can justify its current valuation, but the impact of the capital too big for my overall investment group will be too big. So from the point of view of risk control, to reduce the price and the entire chip section valuation, if it goes to a very high position, then we should make a certain adjustment.

为什么不清仓？

Many people may ask why not clear the warehouse? It depends on each person's investment logic or investment framework. In my own investment framework, the basic face is always the most important. If a company is getting better and better, it may be because of the emotional aspect in the short term, or because of the participation, it may be stubborn to go to a very high position. The high value of the stock price is not a reason to clear the warehouse. I can continue to hold oil and I can reduce the warehouse, but it is absolutely not the reason to clear the warehouse.

Unless in the same block, in the same mark, I can find a replacement mark, then I may clear the warehouse. Otherwise, the value of the stock price goes to the high position, I can reduce the warehouse, but I will not clear the warehouse.

仓位比例决定操作

And a lot of times, whether or not to cut the stock depends on the ratio of this title itself in my overall investment group. So now Intel still accounts for more than 20% of my investment. If today after the increase, Intel only accounts for 2%, then for me, I have no concern at all. On the contrary, it is stubborn and I will continue to hold it. If it falls down, there is any rebound of this rebound of 2% or 30%, I will still add the stock. But now, with my first-generation warehouse, which is above 20%, I am very stubborn and I should take the initiative to do the warehouse inspection.

So it's not a... A lot of times you will find that a site itself, its price, for example, after the price continues to rise to 150 or 160, it depends on the difference in each person's warehouse. I think the operation is actually different.

八、估值高 ≠ 必然下跌：SOX的两段历史

Another point is that from this picture, I want to tell you that I have always emphasized that it is not because the valuation is too high, then the price of this landmark must fall. It's not like that.

I give him two examples. In the same picture, we see that SOX is the entire chip version of the entire chip version of the ETF. The blue line is the valuation, right? Then the white line is the valuation, and the blue line is the price.

You can see that in 2024, the valuation hit 30 times and then the valuation dropped. At that time, the price did drop. Then in August, what happened in July and August? Carriage, a flat, right? Then the valuation did drop. The price also dropped. So in those two periods, the two valuation hits 30 times in 2024, there was a drop in the valuation and there was a rebound in the valuation. Then the price also appeared to rebound.

But let me give you an example in the September of 25. Then until the first half of the 26th year, in March, we saw the blue line — the white line was not as stubborn as it was at the time 30 times. It's about 29 times the position. It started to fall. But what's the price? The price has risen in this period of time.

Why? The price rose and the valuation rose. It means that in the time of these six places, the uptrend of EPS is very large. The price equals the value of EPS times the value of the value of the value.

So many people may not understand that from March this year to now, I only pulled the chip version by one times. So there are bubbles in the chip version. But in fact, if you put this focus on the value, you said that the value of 28.6 times the value has bubbles. If today is 40 times 50 times, I can say that it is bubble. Because I haven't seen it in history. But today, it didn't break the high of 2024. I can't say that the entire chip market has bubbles, even if it has a very large rise in the short term.

Last time, I helped you to disassemble it. This increase of 110% is about 70% from the expansion of the valuation. The remaining 20% is from the uptrend of EPS. So this wave of uptrend is from the expansion of valuation and the uptrend of EPS.

九、估值在高位时的策略：降低预期

Most of the time, the expansion of valuation is going on. The market is moving forward, the expansion of the valuation is moving forward to the future. This is also what I have always emphasized to everyone.

I have mentioned this example of UNH many times. I said that UNH's valuation has returned to an average position in history, and it is relatively high. Then I will not expect a valuation expansion of UNH. If you expect a valuation expansion, it is unreasonable to assume that there may be an overshoot in the short term. Of course, it is possible, including the chip block, and it is the same.

Is it possible to go to 33 or 34 times in the short term? Of course it is possible. If it goes to 33, 34, you know that the valuation has reached a very high position. At this time, you can slowly cut it. It's not that you should expect it to go to 33 or 34 times when it's at 30 times, so you're still all in on this tag. At this time, I think it's completely unreasonable.

So when the valuation is at a high position, we should lower our expectations. Then our expectation is the best situation is OK. The valuation can remain at a high position and then slowly digest through the basic side. And the valuation can remain at a high position. The price can still rise. Where does this rise come from? From the uptrend of EPS increases in the future. But we must lower the expectation of the future price return.

I still point to this example of UNH. The current UNH valuation has returned 21 times, back to 22 times or so. How much is the increase in UNH EPS? The increase in EPS is 12% to 15%. At least 12% to 15% this year. What is my expectation of its price next? It's 12% to 15%. Right? I'm talking about its price expectation. It can rise by 50% in this position now. This is unreasonable. Where can 50% rise? Right.

十、3月低位为何敢买：估值是底层逻辑

I said at the end of March, although the market was very pessimistic at that time, and the market said that the index did not fall much, but I said you go and look at the valuation, you look at the valuation of the big economy, and then you look at the valuation of the chip, which has returned to this level in March of last year, 2025, even if the price did not fall much. Why? Because during this period, the increase in EPS is constantly increasing, and the increase in EPS is constantly increasing. Even if the price does not move, your valuation will decline.

And as long as we keep accumulating warehouse space during that period of time, and then we keep getting it until now, then we know that OK, the valuation is close to the upper limit. When it's close to the upper limit, we slowly slowly cut the warehouse step by step. Basically, it's okay if it doesn't continue to hold. We're waiting for it to return to the valuation. The valuation will definitely return.

You believe me. You just look back 25 years to 26 years. In the past two and a half years, has the valuation fluctuated in the entire range? Of course not. So don't expect anything. The entire new market can be compared to the market all the way up to the market. It's impossible to go back and forth. The valuation will definitely go back and forth.

十一、纪律 vs 小概率事件

We need to find a rule in the market, find a discipline to follow, and not to fight a small probability event at some time.

I will give you another example. For example, if your valuation is 30 times, you say, I think the valuation can go to 33, 34 times, so I will OIN in. It has been 30 times, it is already in a very high position. You say I can, I think this time it can go to 33, 34 times. Is it possible to go? Of course it is possible to go, but is this probability big or not? So is it necessary to expand for the remaining 10% and then OIN? I think it's unnecessary.

On the contrary, the biggest time point of that wave is to expand to nearly 30 times at 16 times, 17 times. This period of time has been taken. This is the most correct way.

But this time it has rebounded to 20 times, 22 times, 23 times. Many people say I want to leave the field. I think this wave of up and down is over. In fact, look at the valuation. Just like the valuation I have been updating with you. I said it has never been so expensive.

十二、当鹰派Fed遇到低估值科技股

How do we put the United Nations? If you also think that the United Nations is the British side this time, the United Nations is the British side of the Kaifeng Walsh. How do you combine the United Nations and the operation of the British side?

In the same way, go back to your entire investment framework. What is the most important for us? For me, the basic face and stubbornness are always the most important. If the basic face and stubbornness, for example, the stubbornness of big technology now, is now in a very reasonable position, and even many big technologies are low. At this time, even if the United Nations is the British side, I will not go to the industry of big technology to do a check. I will not.

But on the contrary, I know a US Fed president of the British Union. If the value of the chip block is low — I know the value of the chip block is already high. Because I know there is a US Fed. There is a US Fed of the British Union over there. So I will do some checks for the chip block, right? So what is the most important thing in investment logic?

When two investment logics collide, for example, how to make this decision between low-quality big technology and the US President of the United States? We have to have a priority judgment standard. In my investment framework, it is always the priority judgment standard. Just like this year in March, it is the same. Including last year in April, it is the same. The reason why they were able to buy in or dare to buy in at that position and ignore some of the story lines of the market at that time is because they obeyed the underlying logic or the most important logic line is the basic value.

十三、Seeking Alpha 推广

OK, the last thing I want to share with you today is Seeking Alpha. This event of 75 people in the whole field, including their alpha picks, the volatilized stock selection function, the standard 500 to now 11% of YTT, their alpha picks have increased by 40%. In the past three months, the criteria for selecting these labels have been relatively high and relatively good. Some of the labels have been rewarded more than 30%.

So if you are interested, you can find their chain of Alphapix in the top-down comments. Including their premium membership, and the subscription of the combo. So this event, the 75th of the whole game, is almost the last event of the year. Everyone should be familiar with Seeking Alpha. If you are interested, you can find the subscription link in the top-down comments.

结语

That's all for today. See you next time.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：鲍威尔（Kevin Walsh）这次"放权"，实质是把美联储从"舵手"降级成"乘客"
视频里小淘把Powell（视频转录里被翻译成"Kevin Walsh"）那句"让市场自己去解读数据"轻轻带过，但这其实是一次**货币政策范式的静默政变**。
过去几十年里，美联储花了巨大的政治资本去训练市场"听Fed的"——前瞻指引、点阵图、Fed Speakers轮番出场push back，全部目的是**让Fed的叙事压倒市场的叙事**。而这次主席说"你们看市场怎么解读就好"，等于公开承认：**Fed不再有信息优势，也不再有叙事垄断权**。
这背后藏着一个更深的政治结构问题：在特朗普高压、内部19人分歧极大（8人不加、剩余分散）的情况下，主席选择"不交SEP"+"听市场的"，其实是一种**博弈论意义上的甩锅策略**——把降息/加息的责任从"我决定"变成"市场逼我"。这跟一个CEO在董事会分裂时说"我们做用户调研决定"是同一种心理动作。
## 二、跨领域连接：两年期美债，是金融市场里的"前额叶皮层"
视频反复用一张图说"白线（2Y收益率）领先蓝线（联邦基金利率）"。

item.title: [Latent Space] The AI Frontier: from FLOPs to Megawatts — Anjney Midha, AMP
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Anthropic 的"穷"才是它的护城河

大多数人把 Anthropic 在编程领域的突破归因于"幸运的骰子"——某次训练 run 偶然出了好结果，然后他们重仓投入。但 Anjney 给出了一个完全相反的解读：Anthropic 的胜利不是因为运气好，而是因为它早期被迫贫穷。

「Luck favors the prepared mind」——他引用童年老师的话指出，那些被 OpenAI 抢走、被投资人拒绝的"21 个 No"恰恰是 Anthropic 文化的塑造者。没有钱、没有资源的窘迫，逼迫他们必须定义 P0（最高优先级），而 P0 一旦被定义就成为文化基石：「If we crack coding, we crack AGI」。

这里有一个让人脊背发凉的反向推论：今天那些拿到几十亿美元、却"做不出 soda"的 AI 实验室，问题恰恰在于他们太富有了。没有 21 个 No、没有匮乏、没有共同熬过的危机，文化就无法被淬炼出锋利的边界。资本过剩本身正在成为这一代 AI 公司的隐性诅咒。

二、跨领域连接：电网的历史 = AI 算力的未来 = 人类临终关怀的同构问题

Anjney 把自己定位为算力领域的 ISO（独立系统运营商），原型是 1900 年代北美电网的 PJM Interconnection。这本身已经够有趣了，但更深的洞见在于——他在电网、Anthropic 文化、印度对死亡的观念、临终医疗这四个看似毫无关联的领域里，反复使用了同一个分析框架：

领域 失效模式 根因
算力集群 MFU 30%，节点利用率不足 资本→部署→管理者之间"度数偏差"在 scale 中被放大
AI 实验室 烧钱却不出货 资金充裕导致 P0 模糊，文化无张力
美国临终医疗 30% Medicare 花在最后阶段 医生→法律责任→患者的信任传递断裂，导致"什么都试一下"的过度治疗
DeepMind 研究 6 个月禁运期后只发布"不够好"的论文 商业团队与研究者的对齐失败，造成逆向选择

所有这些问题的本质都是同一个：在抽象层之间的信任边界（trust boundary）上发生了"通信损耗"。 这也是为什么他说 Matrox 的 Reiner 选择 Nvidia 参考架构是聪明的——因为减少了一层 trust boundary 的协商成本。

意外之处在于：他从印度教对死亡的庆祝观（街上跳舞、解脱的旅程），推导出美国医疗系统的系统性失效——文化先验（死亡是终点）→ 医生防御性诊断 → 信任损耗 → 30% GDP 的浪费。这是一个真正打破学科边界的诊断。

三、隐藏的系统真相：你以为的"对齐"问题，本质是"度数偏差"问题

最值得收藏的一个比喻是 "radian metaphor"：两条线在起点只差几度，scale 到远处就分道扬镳。

这个比喻可以解释这期播客里几乎所有现象： - 为什么大厂里 95% 利用率被视为标准、外面却没人达到？——资本与执行之间的角度偏差。 - 为什么 Anthropic 能从「safety」一词坚持五年？——他们在第 0 天就把那个角度校准了。 - 为什么 SF Compute 的期货市场难以启动？——多方信任边界上的角度偏差累积。 - 为什么 1.3 GW 算力扩到 6 GW 需要 ISO？——不是物理瓶颈，是协调瓶颈。

真正的洞见在这里：Anjney 说「culture is not a set of beliefs, it's a set of actions」，对应到工程上就是——对齐不是一次性声明，而是每天的角度校准。这与他对 Anthropic 的诊断完美闭环：Anthropic 不是"信仰"safety，而是每天用一个个小决策（哪怕被嘲笑、哪怕错过窗口）把那个角度校准回零。

如果把这套框架反推到读者自己的人生：你今天看似无关紧要的选择，正在以"几度的偏差"决定五年后你出现在哪里。Anjney 用电网和 GPU 讲的故事，最终其实是一个关于「复利性偏航」的普适物理学。

番外彩蛋：一个被掩盖的产业级警告

视频里一句被一带而过的数据——「今年美国 20% 的新数据中心可能因社区反对建不起来」——是整个 AI scaling 叙事里最被低估的瓶颈。Scott Nolan 提出的"每小时多收 50 美分返还给社区"是一个典型的机制设计破局：它不是技术问题，是把 negative externality 内化的经济学问题。这里隐藏着未来 5 年最大的政治-技术交叉机会，但几乎没人在认真讨论。

📝 转录文本

开场：文化的脆弱性

But more concretely, there are so many AI labs today that have all the cash they need. They have all the compute they need and they're still not able to ship anything soda. And then you start seeing people leave and so on. And my diagnosis is — it's the culture. If you stop taking the actions that demonstrate the mission alignment to what you've said to your team and to the world matters to you, then your culture starts to fray.

主持人致谢

Before we get into today's episode, I just have a small message for listeners. Thank you. We would not be able to bring you the AI engineering, science, and entertainment content that you so clearly want if you didn't choose to also click in and tune into our content.

We've been approached by sponsors on an almost daily basis, but fortunately enough of you actually subscribe to us to keep all this sustainable without ads. And we want to keep it that way.

But I just have one favor to ask all of you. The single most powerful, completely free thing you can do is to click that subscribe button. It's the only thing I'll ever ask of you and it means absolutely everything to me and my team that works so hard to bring the week. If you do it, I promise you we'll never stop working to make this show even better. Now let's get into it.

一、集群利用率：从 MFU 到领导力问题

We're in Periodic Labs with Anjney Midha, CEO founder of Amp. Welcome. Thanks for having me.

At Google, if you utilize — there's two types of utilization usually, right, that you're measuring in these clusters. One is node allocation and the other is MFU. Node utilization is usually like what percentage of cards in the data center are just used. And if it's not at like 95%—

There's no excuse.

There's no excuse, right? I think 95% at Google, which is where my co-founder Seb came from — he built the Borg / X-Borg / GQM scheduler at Google. There I think 95% is considered an outage. So 96% node utilization should be standard. And most single-down clusters are not running at that. So that's one. And then MFU utilization should be — I would say the best in class today somewhere between 60 and 70%.

I think it's a leadership question, right? And fundamentally it's an alignment question: are the people who are funding the cluster and then deploying the cluster actually aligned? Sometimes theoretically they are, but in practice the number of people in the chain — the supply chain between the capital and all the way to whoever's managing the cluster and then whoever's measuring what the output is — are just so many degrees of separation away.

弧度比喻

Have you ever heard that sort of radian metaphor? At the beginning of an arc, if you have two lines that are just off by a few degrees, it spreads out at scale. And I think what's happening is a lot of cluster implementations and a lot of frontier labs initialize the plan — kind of a North Star with a team that wants to do good — but then they're required to scale so fast instead of iteratively that the wastage just compounds really fast at scale.

So I think we know the answer, which is: just do iterative bring-ups. If you spend time with people who've been in the semiconductor industry or the DSA industry for a long time, this is not new. And I don't think AI should be an excuse. Sure, we have a lot of new capabilities, but that doesn't mean just abandon common sense. Common sense should always be in fashion.

AI scaling doesn't change that. In fact, if anything, AI scaling should be putting a premium on the value of common sense and infrastructure because the margin of error now is so much lower and the cost of wastage is so much higher. And the cost of wastage, by the way, is not just economic.

I'm an investor by background. Over the last few years now we're running an AI infrastructure business called Amp, and I think that it's okay to say this time is different on the capabilities front. We are genuinely getting capabilities of a kind we haven't had before. That doesn't give you an excuse to say this time is different for everything — especially infrastructure.

I love the hacker mindset and the hustler mindset — that's great for the startup mindset. But you remember this moment where Zuck went from saying "move fast, break things" to—

Move faster, stable infrastructure.

Move faster, stable infrastructure. I think now we need to move fast with responsible infrastructure.

二、数据中心与社区：把外部性内化

They're going to say, "Where is the impact?" In our class yesterday, Scott Nolan, who's the founder of General Matter, came by at Stanford to speak about energy bottlenecks. And he had a phenomenal idea.

He said: if you look at the marginal unit economics of compute per hour — let's call it $4 an hour — if you're having to bring up a new data center in a new community, why not just say we're going to charge $4.50 an hour, and that marginal increase we just literally take and give it to the local community as cash.

I can tell you as a customer of that compute, I would love that. I'd be happy to pay an additional 50 cents per hour at scale.

Wow, yeah.

Because if that means the public benefit is so clear to the communities that the data centers are coming up in, I'm going to feel like that compute is much more reliable. Up to 20% of all data centers this year in the US, my understanding is, are at risk.

Of community backlash?

Of not getting the community support they need to get brought up.

Wow. That's a huge number.

Yeah. Now, we should dig into what that number is — it can get over-reported. But—

They don't just care about jobs. They care about all the other stuff around it — power grid, environment—

Permitting and so on. Imagine if you said: there's a new AI deal — if we're bringing up a data center in your community, we're actually going to reduce the cost of your electricity bill. Okay, now we're talking. The community is going, "Okay, now this is a deal. I feel like a partner in this." Right now, that's not happening.

There will be audits. There will be investigations. And when the regulators come, I don't know when it's going to be, but the folks who are moving fast and breaking things in the name of AI progress better be prepared.

That's certainly not how we're procuring compute. We're trying as much as we can to work with partners who have long-term track records. Many of whom, by the way, are not AI providers. I think this whole idea of "neo-clouds" being somehow a new category is a lot of marketing speak. There are really good, reliable, trusted data center providers in America who've been around 20-plus years.

Sure, are they sponsoring happy hours at NeurIPS? No. Are they legibly "bitter-lesson built"? No. Are they hanging out in my "situationally aware" parties? No. But they're adults. I trust them. They can run land. They can run power. They have credit histories. We sit down, we have conversations. Many of them live in Silicon Valley. They've had to deal with the boom and bust cycles of the internet. I love those folks. They are stable infrastructure partners and thinkers.

There's a lot of short-term thinking going on in the compute layer. And it's going to catch up to us. It's not going to be good.

三、Amp 的设计哲学：算力网格 = 电网的 ISO

You talk about aligning incentives. I would think that aligning incentives means you have the full stack in one company — which is XAI and OpenAI. So why are you, as a standalone infrastructure layer, somehow more aligned to your portfolio companies than people who just own the whole thing?

In systems design, there are two regimes of architecture: integration, and pooling-and-utilization. The way to increase utilization often is — you can do systems integration where you collapse a lot of processes into one node, or you can pull out a process from a node and share that resource amongst several different nodes.

So we see the Amp Grid, which is the system we're building here — basically a compute grid. We're trying to do for compute what the electric grid did for electricity. This is a pooling-and-utilization layer across clouds. So we're actually the opposite of a full-stack integration. It's much more horizontal. It's multi-cloud, multi-silicon.

The goal is to try to make megaflops flow like megawatts.

That is very hard to do today for many reasons. There are stranded pools of compute all over the place and there's no fungibility. Right now we do it at the level of scheduling, and we often do it at the economic layer. But as we start to announce what we're working on, it's extraordinary how many folks are coming out of the woodwork saying, "Hey, I'm actually working on a way to make compute fungible at this part of the stack and that part of the stack." As a grid, we'd like all of these folks to participate.

Neo-clouds 不是供应商，是需求方

People will often ask me, "Anjney, you're a neo-cloud?" I go: "No, actually, neo-clouds are demand — off-takers of the grid." We see ourselves as what's called an independent system operator (ISO).

If you study the history of the electric grid: once it became legible to factories and industrial participants that pooling was a good idea — "we should pool our generators instead of all having half a generator running at half capacity in our backyard" — there was a need for an independent entity who could coordinate all these parties: transmission lines, power generation facilities, factories. That neutral coordination mechanism is very critical.

The most enduring grids were those that never owned their own assets. They often started with long-term anchors who were uncorrelated sources of demand — a steel factory or shoe mill in a particular town who weren't competitive: the steel factory wanted to spike up at night, the shoe mill wanted to spike up during the day. So then you pool and you share. Each is guaranteed some base load, but you schedule your spikes to drive a peak utilization across the town.

The gold standard historically has been utility companies like PJM Interconnection in the Northeast of America, which over many years became this ISO of the grid. That's how we see ourselves economically.

从调度层切入

From a technical perspective, we started at the scheduling layer because Seb and Mihai, who run engineering here, did that at Google.

And you have infra folks from Discord as well.

I don't know if Discord is the primary identity, but — I was running the developer platform there. The internal infrastructure I was not responsible for; that was actually a guy by the name of Mark Smith who was extraordinary.

Discord is actually a counter-example. I had the chance to learn a lot about fully-full-stack infra there because Discord built its own WebRTC — voice and video infra. Discord did not use third-party infra for communication. It was all built in-house. And then the way you maximize utilization was you pulled demand from the world's 200-million-plus monthly active gamers.

In system design, the two concepts that keep coming up over and over again are abstraction and composition — verticalization and horizontalization, bundling and unbundling.

In that sense, Amp is an independent system operator of the grid. We pool supply from a number of partners we trust at about 1.3 gigawatt scale over 4 years. And then we pool demand from some of the world's best research labs. We're sitting at one — Periodic Labs — who need extraordinary long-term demand. The idea is that each of them is guaranteed base load on the grid, but they can spike up and down flexibly on compute with much shorter timelines as needed.

That was roughly the design of the program I came up with at A16Z called Oxygen. Same design as the GQM / Borg / X-Borg implementation at Google that Mihai and Seb built — how do you allow teams inside of Google on internal infrastructure to be guaranteed capacity for their base workloads, but when they need to spike up on research, ensure that was sufficiently there?

And the big innovation that was implemented in that infra space maybe 3-4 years ago at Google was the idea of interruptible demand. You queue up a bunch of jobs, and through a credit system there can be a bidding mechanism — dynamic prioritization. Jobs can get interrupted: "I have 10 credits I want to spend on this job; another research lead — Genie 3 or whatever — is only worth 5 credits; Nano Banana 2 is worth 10 credits, so Nano Banana gets priority." That's a made-up example.

It's very real. Brain Marketplace was real. We've covered this on the pod with David.

And the criticism is that sometimes you need central command to go all-in on the thing — sometimes capitalism via credits doesn't work.

Not a criticism of Amp — I'm saying this is a thing that has been tried internally within Google and it led to Google missing GPT.

四、Amp 的公司结构：控股公司模型

We structured ourselves essentially very similarly to Google. We are structured as a holdings company. Alphabet Holdings has subsidiaries called Google and "Other Bets." We've got Amp Holdings, our infrastructure business, and we've got a capital business called Foundry that incubates new frontier AI labs and invests in them as venture capital — like Periodic. We put a few hundred million dollars into Anthropic from our fund earlier this year.

Wherever we feel like teams are making progress — especially researchers who push the frontier inside of existing labs like DeepMind — there comes a point where they feel misaligned with the "dictatorship" of Alphabet Holdings. Sometimes the dictatorship doesn't want them anymore: "Thank you. You've helped us through the zero-to-one phase, and for whatever reason we're going to deprioritize your amazing omni-model and prioritize coding."

I think that's a tragedy, but I get it. Sergey and Demis are running their own business there. But that doesn't mean the rest of us should sit around waiting for that progress to get unlocked for the rest of the world and humanity. If you think about how much extraordinary research has happened inside of DeepMind over the last 10 years — Demis and Sergey did such a great job — but at the end of the day, so much of that has never seen the light of day.

Or it's papers only, never shipped into production.

What's worse — the paper is actually not even being published anymore because there's a six-month embargo inside of DeepMind. A paper comes out, and if anybody on the business team says "this could be interesting," it's embargoed for life. So the stuff that gets published is the stuff that's not good enough. There's an adverse selection problem.

It's a common complaint at NeurIPS: "Why would I look at the papers that are the trash of GDM?"

I think it's a tragedy. There are negative externalities of research being hoarded. That's a market failure. And somebody needs to unlock that research. We can't do it on our own — we only have 1.3 gigawatts of compute. That's nothing. That's about $40 billion of cloud spend. We're going to need a little more.

That's a new number I haven't come across. That's huge.

Yeah. And to be clear, we haven't secured all of it. That's how much demand we have started to secure. Publicly we haven't actually confirmed how much we have for this year.

目标规模

Where do you want to get to?

The steady state would be that we have a base load pool of 1.3 gigawatts at all times. For spike capacity, right now my estimate is we need roughly 6 gigawatts over the next 4 years for all our teams to feel like they were able to keep moving the frontier — whether it's superconductor discovery here, or a new investment we're working on in the end-of-life prediction space in healthcare.

五、个人故事：临终预测与跨文化的死亡观

This was actually my graduate school work. I went to graduate school for bioinformatics at Stanford Med.

Econ, MCS, bio.

I was this really weird cat where I was never satisfied with my major options. At one point I was an econ major, then a CS major, then an MCS major (Mathematical Computational Science). They decided to end that major, so I took all that coursework and applied it to grad school — a master's in bioinformatics. I thought I was going to do a PhD. I never ended up doing it. I dropped out and went to work at Kleiner.

But I was lucky enough to apprentice with this professor at Stanford Med — Nigam Shah — and he was working on end-of-life prediction. Stanford is one of the only research facilities in America that has a longitudinal patient dataset at scale — at least 12 million patient lives. The only larger dataset is at the VA. To do any deep learning on that dataset (called the STRIDE dataset at the time), you had to be a Stanford Med School affiliate, which is why I enrolled in the bioinformatics department.

Deep learning was early. Nigam Shah had the vision to see you could do end-of-life prediction to help palliative care. In America, over 30% of all Medicare/Medicaid spend, at least at that time, was spent on end-of-life care.

印度文化中的死亡

We grew up in Asia, so I have a very different relationship with death than I find folks who grew up in America do. In America, spiritually and culturally — especially in Western societies where the Christian tradition frames death as this terminal point, with a judgment day — we view death with a finality.

In Indian culture, in Hindu culture, death is—

Buddhist as well.

You're a Buddhist, yeah. So it's one step in a journey of many lives.

I grew up in Chennai in the south of India, and when people die, you dance on the street. There's a procession where your body is carried to be cremated, and your family celebrates. There are drums. The idea is that you're going to be reincarnated. You've been liberated from the responsibilities of this life, and now you're on to your next. It's like going off to a new college.

So it was so alien to me when I got here as an undergrad that the medical system works backwards from that assumption — that we have to view death as this terminal thing and delay it, postpone it. It's a bad thing.

临床决策支持的困境

At the time, clinical decision support in the United States was a very primitive field. Even to this day, physicians in the United States will tell you when you have a terminal disease: "We've diagnosed you. You have somewhere between 6 months to 6 years to live." What do you do with that information? The error bars are so high.

In times of uncertainty, we default to culture. And when the culture is "this is a bad thing, I've got to prolong my life," then you start doing things like —

From a systems perspective, what's going on there: physicians often feel they need to provide such high bars because there's always uncertainty in end-of-life diagnosis. And if you provide the wrong recommendation, you can be sued for medical malpractice. Your license can be taken away. It can be catastrophic for your career.

In contrast, in countries where that's not the case, physicians are quite prescriptive: "Hey, this is your condition. The literature says you probably have this much time on Earth left. My expert opinion is you're an outlier or whatever." That empowers a patient. "I trust my doctor. They said on average I have 6 months to live. But if I do these things, I may have a shot because of my predispositions or genetic history." That empowers you to go about your life in a more scientific way than leaning on religion, culture, spirituality.

Here, because of medical malpractice looming overhead, a physician never gives you a clear recommendation. So you say, "Okay doc, let's try it all." You start a whole regime of drugs and therapies. You spend weeks in the hospital. That deteriorates your quality of life. Instead of spending your last few days doing the things you love with your family, you're spending it on a hospital bed. And that ends up being 30% of Medicare/Medicaid. It's worse for the patients. The doctors feel terrible. The American taxpayers pay a huge amount of money.

试图用 AI 破局

Nigam Shah said: "If there's — " I sat down with him, I was 21, I was like, "I want to work on a big problem." He's like, "The big problem is end-of-life care."

We tried to do deep learning on the STRIDE patient datasets — could you have an AI system make a recommendation that is orders of magnitude more precise about how much time you have left, once you've been diagnosed with a terminal condition, than a human? If we can get that precision to be high enough, then you can empower the patient.

It turns out the tech works. Once you get the dataset, RL works. Honestly, even regression models work. You don't need to get that fancy. Half the time we were just running very simple neural nets. Today, what we can do with RL is extraordinary.

The problem remains then and now: regulatory. You actually can't shift the burden of the wrong clinical diagnosis from the physician to the AI system. At that time, 12 years ago, I got quite disillusioned because I didn't have the resources to influence regulation.

Today I'm very lucky. I've been spending a lot of time on my next incubation: how can we unlock patient empowerment by training AI models to do end-of-life prediction with much more precision.

Oh wow. You're still focused on this?

I haven't been able to get this out of my mind a single day for the last 14 years. This is the hill I would like to die on. — Actually, I prefer not to die.

[laughter]

Two issues that should be bipartisan in America: 1. How do we empower patients to make the right clinical decisions at the end of their life, reducing the taxpayer burden with science? 2. Net-positive data centers — because that's the biggest critical bottleneck on training good-enough AI models to help people at the end of their life.

These are two sides of the same scaling bottleneck curve. We formed Amp as a public benefit corporation. My wife and I — her passion is education. Her family is a long line of educators and physicists. This class is my attempt at being an educator, the black sheep of the family. If I'm not educating, the thing I would be doing is working on these two problems — whether on the political spectrum or as a researcher back at some lab. My hope is if anyone listening is passionate about either of those two topics, I'd love to hear from them.

六、Frontier Systems：output-maxing 的学科

You said this is a discipline you want to form — variously called "frontier systems," "one-person frontier lab." What is the ideal name? Is there a broader theme — maybe "anti-waste" — wasting GPUs, wasting human Medicare?

From an engineering perspective, it's basically output-maxing. It's the Department of Output Maxing.

I'm a huge believer in optimal outcomes. In America and other countries, we are losing our appreciation for nuance. This is the thing with AI: "Oh, the bitter lesson holds." Okay, fine. But that doesn't mean you just throw 500,000 GB300s at your suboptimal model scaling and waste a bunch of compute. It also doesn't mean the most optimal is having 50 different architectures where there isn't enough standardization.

One of the reasons Anthropic has had extraordinary velocity is because they picked the Transformer architecture and said "this is simple, let's double down on it." Now luckily there's enough investment in the space that we can afford other architectures, but at the time investment was just too fragmented. So arguably that unlocked scaling.

I think we all owe it to ourselves to do output-maxing with a new capability called AI on a global level.

对齐作为系统问题

If I was starting a new department at Stanford, I'd probably call it the Department of Alignment.

It's an overloaded term.

But alignment really is a hard problem. Full-stack alignment is super hard in any organization. In a VC firm, if you can have full-stack alignment between your limited partners, the founders creating the value, and ultimately the public that owns the IPO stock — that is a gift that keeps giving.

When you study the history of these systems, they usually start out small-scale where the feedback loop is so tight that there's alignment. The more you try to scale, the more division of labor happens, more specialization, and at each step you add abstractions. Wherever there's an API interface, there's communication loss.

A really cool thing would be to figure out: is there a way for us to scale up and scale out without losing any alignment — without lossy transmission?

I mean — standards.

Standards is one way. The other way is you just have net-new capability. What we're trying to do here is discover new superconductors. A room-temperature superconductor would be a lossless transmission mechanism for energy. We would have flying cars within a few years.

So those are the two paths: either standardize on protocols/API specs that allow lossless communication, or come with a whole new capability that unlocks so much abundance that standardization doesn't matter. This is what I spend my days thinking about.

七、SF Compute 与算力期货市场

We don't have time to go into it, but we've done an episode with SF Compute that is trying to standardize the futures contract for compute.

I think Evan is awesome and SF Compute is the kind of effort I hope we can accelerate. These exchanges are really hard to bootstrap. There are many inefficiencies between parties: trust-boundary inefficiencies (one part of the stack doesn't trust another to give visibility), capital markets inefficiencies, operational inefficiencies.

If you can inject a single shock to the system — a ton of compute demand or supply — you can accelerate these new flywheels. My hope is that one day, if SF Compute has excess capacity, they just hook it up to the grid and get flooded with demand from us. On the other side, if they have a ton of demand but no supply, they again hook up to the grid. It's a two-way protocol.

I don't think we're too far from that today. Our working implementation is mostly through a group of labs, universities, and a few trusted parties who all feel like they're in alignment — to borrow an overused word. Our hope is to just have it be an open protocol.

Hook up for demand or for supply?

Both. Unfortunately, what's happened the last 6 weeks is — we thought we'd have a bunch of excess capacity by the end of this year. It's all gone.

It's exploding.

Yeah. My text messages are full of friends — founders who've raised billions of dollars in San Francisco going, "Any chance you have 15 nodes in the next few weeks?"

八、Matrox 与系统协同设计

What is the scope for non-Nvidia? You have Lisa Su coming, and Reiner Pope as well. There's a lot of demand for more performance, alternative architectures. At the same time, this hurts your standardization.

I don't think so. Reiner's a great example. Reiner's the CEO and founder of Matrox. I had him by for office hours in the class earlier today. There was an insight he brought up I hadn't considered before: when they decided to pick the standard for their data center, they picked the Nvidia reference architecture. So Matrox chips just plug into any site that has an Nvidia bring-up plan.

It's just software then. Not the hardware.

From an IO perspective, it's the same footprint as an Nvidia rack. Where they've innovated a bunch is on systems co-design — which is where a lot of the gains are to be had.

He was like: "Anjney, there's just so much work to do when you're building a new chip company. Can't fight on every front." My question to him was, "You're working on this new chip — their tape-out is next year. Who are you going to partner with to host the chips?" He said, "Whoever will host them, that's not my focus."

He decided he didn't want to be a fully integrated chip provider. The bottleneck they're focused on is the logic die. He feels they can crank out a ton of performance gains through co-design there. But that means he delegates the data center provider to a different part of the stack. So he's dependent on that part of the ecosystem to host his chips to get the performance gains to the customer. Now you have another abstraction. You might have loss.

So I asked him: how do you prevent loss? He said: "I just picked the Nvidia standard because I wanted to piggyback off an existing protocol. What's great about Nvidia is that reference architecture is known. It's open. They've published it. So Jensen's actually enabled someone like Reiner to build a chip company like Matrox."

I don't see them as competitive. The compute demand is so high — Nvidia's not able to meet the demand of production. So we just need more chips. I think it's very smart what Matrox has done: "We're not going to innovate on the data center design — thank you Jensen, you've done all the hard work. Where we can innovate is somewhere else." That's very healthy. That's how we unlock new bottlenecks.

Trust boundary 与协同设计

For chip teams like Matrox who have arrived at the insight that co-design is the way, the primary bottleneck is trust boundary. To do co-design well, you need visibility into the next model generation as soon as possible — because it takes two years to tape out. If by the time I bring my chip to market your model architecture has changed, I'm toast.

When Reiner was inside Google, he was sitting next to the Gemini team — on Palm or whatever.

His co-founder was one of the Palm guys, I think.

Yes. When you're inside the trust boundary of Google, your systems co-design loop is super tight. When you leave as a founder, one of the biggest risks is now you're outside the trust boundary.

So what I love doing is helping chip teams who can unlock more capacity for the independent ecosystem get access to trust. Because if I've been involved with a lab from day one — I was lucky enough to work with Anthropic, I'm on the board of Mistral, I helped Black Forest Labs get started — at this point I'm on six or seven different teams.

Only six? My mental number was 13.

I go deep with one at a time.

You were founding CEO of Arena?

No, that was an administrative 5-month gig where Waylon and Anastasia were graduating from their PhDs. They didn't need a product team. I helped recruit the head of engineering, product, and design. But Anastasia has always been the CEO of that company. I played a pinch-hitting job. I was CEO intern for 5 months.

九、研究者作为创始人

I interviewed Anastasios. He's very well-spoken — a former debate champion, but also very quantitative and mathematical. Such a unicorn.

If you look at his output, he's an output-maxer. By the time he was graduating from his PhD — which he only graduated last year — he had published more work with a citation count than people twice his age. At the same time, he'd already started a project called LM Arena that was being used by millions of people as a side project.

Time and time again, what I've realized is venture capitalists suck at seeing human beings as dynamic agents. They want to put you in a box: "This is your thing."

The first time I got introduced to Anastasios, somebody told me, "Oh, he's amazing, but he's a researcher."

Not a CEO.

I was like, "Are you crazy? Have you met Dario? Dario is a scientist. He's gone from zero to what will soon be a trillion-dollar company in 4 years."

Being a CEO, nominally speaking, is not that hard. Being a good CEO is hard. Being a great CEO actually requires a level of performance that scientists who have already published at the top of their field have accomplished. It is super hard to be a competitive scientist. To publish in academia over the last 20-30 years, to make it to the top of your discipline at a place like Berkeley — you were a star athlete. You're an athlete of the mind.

Whether you're Anastasios or Waylon at Berkeley, or Robin who with Blackforest created Stable Diffusion, or Guillaume at Meta who created Llama before he started Mistral — the amount of human leadership you have to demonstrate to get the resources, get the trust of the organization, publish it, put it out — I would just fund researchers all day.

If they've put soda out there, they're star athletes already. If they haven't, look — they can still be good CEOs, but then the failure mode is they just don't want to be CEOs. They primarily want to publish. And that's okay too.

One of the things we do with the Amp Grid is donate excess compute to nonprofits, like university labs. We carved out a couple thousand H100s. There's extraordinary research being done on university campuses. My father-in-law is a physicist — extraordinary work in physics, and we need that.

But if you want to be a CEO, you need to be willing to be confrontational outside of science. Within the scientific community, some of the best researchers are very confrontational about their convictions — "this architecture is right." To be a great CEO, you have to be willing to be confrontational up and down the stack — to your own team, hiring, recruiting, customers. Pretty much everyone.

十、Stakes、信任与社区

I can't imagine the stakes that Dario has had to go through.

I don't think the stakes are that different from how you're feeling it. Stakes are personal scaling vectors. Things that seem so low to you — like having this podcast, where you can talk to somebody and have a conversation — I mean, you're an extraordinary communicator. Already in this conversation, you've pulled more out of me than most people. And I've been on 12 podcasts in the last 2 weeks.

We've just seen each other enough that there's some base trust. And I know that trust is a big deal for you.

Trust is about consistency. We've seen each other in the community for years. I don't know — the first time we met was at NeurIPS in New Orleans.

I remember it. Raiko had set up this —

Raiko's amazing. He set up this luncheon.

Yeah, I was like, "Who's this Discord guy?"

No, you were much less polite. You were like, "Who's this VC?" You made some investment.

Oh my god, I'm so sorry. I didn't know who you were.

The introduction was bad. But then I think I heard your accent — "Are you Singaporean?" "Yeah, I went to high school JC in Singapore." And then the ice broke.

This is the thing about context. In the scientific community sometimes the stakes are very high for people who haven't had the EQ coaching and mentorship. To have scientific impact, you often need to be extraordinarily emotionally in tune with the folks you're trying to influence. What comes so naturally to you is actually a super high-stakes thing to other people. I wouldn't assume that Dario is more stressed out than you. You'll be surprised how similar and small the problems are that some of the world's biggest leaders are facing.

That's what I've learned from this class. The guest speakers — Sam, Satya, Jensen — they are Coachella. We got to get all the headliners. I'm very lucky some of these people have either mentored me over the years or I've done business with them. When you take the performative stuff out — and any assumptions you may have about these people that you read in the press or on Twitter — we're all just humans. We're all trying to get along.

What's so special about this moment is AI — scaling, the bitter lesson — is forcing a lot of people to revise their assumptions for how the world works and go back to first principles.

I was at an event last week in Texas and ran into somebody who said, "Anjney, I came across the class. What do you think about real-time action prediction models?" I can't tell you how happy it made me feel when they asked me that question. I know they've done the work. They've challenged the assumption. They didn't ask me, "What do you think of world models?" World models are cool and everything, but you and I both know that is a layer of abstraction that is sometimes not usefully precise enough.

There's like four different kinds of world models.

Exactly.

We've done the pod with General Intuition, by the way.

Oh cool. I love Pim. He's great.

What I love about people who've done that level of work: they realize they're not in competition with people the rest of the world thinks they're in competition with. They're focused on their mission and they have a systems understanding of the bottleneck they're trying to solve. When somebody else says, "I'm working on real-time action prediction models too," Pim goes, "Oh, I love that person. I can learn from them." But the minute they're like, "Oh, that person's a world-model person," it's like, "Ah, which type of world-model person?"

For people who do the work, who can be usefully precise about what is actually going on in the world of frontier research, the sense of camaraderie is still real and alive. But it gets lost sometimes when you have to abstract the technical complexities in business terms — and then the VCs are like, "How are you different from that world-model company?"

十一、领导 vs 获胜

The misalignment — I think people listening get a sense of what it's like to operate at a real level, rather than the journalist level where you have to put everyone in a rough category and create a narrative of competition.

This idea of "winning" is so weird to me.

You do want to win. You want to compete with us.

No, I think you want to lead. You want to push the frontier. You want to push the state of the art. You want to do something that hasn't been done before. You want to capture value. But you don't want to capture so much value that people think you're unaligned with your mission. You want to capture enough value that you can keep innovating.

People want to lead. Again, I love Jensen. He's a leader. The mindset he talked about on Dwarkesh's podcast — "I didn't wake up with a loser mindset" — was awesome. He's an engineer. Dwarkesh has done the work. Even though to me it was very obvious they were talking about the same thing, they just passed each other. Jensen has this five-layer cake abstraction of how the industry works. Dwarkesh from that podcast had more of a pre-training, mid-training, post-training systems loop concept.

It's just a factor of who he talks to.

It's the abstraction, the mental models — so much of the problem in the world is reasoning by analogy, and then the assumptions that are held invisibly.

I've said this is the best time in human history for first-principle thinkers, because everything you think will happen is actually now coming true.

Correct. The VC community is notorious for this. In times of uncertainty, people cling to axioms that end up being true from the previous era. They proclaim them with confidence as if they're truths, but they're not. It's very important to see the distinction between a heuristic and an axiom.

An axiom can be proven from internal consistency. A heuristic is a shortcut. The number of people I've had to put up with over the last few years who use heuristics as axioms to judge people, to judge which companies will succeed — "Oh yeah, Anthropic, they're just training models right now" — which over the fullness of time, if you squint, maybe. But the way you arrive there is so important that you can dismiss people.

十二、Anthropic 的"准备好的运气"

Here's what happened. Anthropic basically achieved takeoff in October of last year. That training run—

Whatever — F37?

I forget the numbers, but whatever that checkpoint was — those of us in the community, especially once post-training was done and it was released in December—

We saw it at Cognition.

Right. Can I sneak a sneaky question in there? The number one question is: how did Anthropic crack coding? Because Claude 1, Claude 2 — okay, it was part of it, but it wasn't a big deal. And the leading hypothesis is it was a lucky dice roll that was then compounded — like it was mildly better, but then they saw it and were like, "Okay, let's really invest."

I had this very annoying teacher. I went to this boarding school called Rishi Valley in India — like this bird preserve, 350 acres in rural India. There was no technology for 7 years. There was this teacher — I won't name them — who would say (and I hated every time he said this): "Luck favors the prepared mind."

The way he delivered it always grated me. I was one of those kids who got a good grade without trying very hard — high school and middle school is not that hard if you're paying attention. I would get an 80% grade, and he would keep pushing me: "The reason you didn't get 95-plus is because you're not that lucky." I'd say, "What do you mean?" He'd say, "You didn't have a prepared mind."

There was one time I got 95-96 on a subject. I felt entitled. "I'm going to keep doing this." And I didn't. He was like, "Luck favors a prepared mind. You got lucky last time, but you got to stay prepared."

I didn't understand what he meant. Now that I'm older — these adults actually knew a thing or two.

Anthropic has been the most prepared company for 4 years. When the right context data comes in, the right developers start sending in the right context diffs — sure, you could say they got lucky. But if you ask me, they're pretty damn prepared, with paranoia, for 4 years. It was so hard for them to get going early on that they had to do so much more with so much less that you just have to be prepared to be that efficient.

Yes. There's numbers on their burn compared to OpenAI. I've written about it. They are so much more efficient in the way—

Not even close. They've been prepared to output-max for the world. You could call that luck, but luck favors a prepared mind.

十三、文化作为护城河（且脆弱）

One of the things in your old lectures — you were like, "People think data is a moat. Actually it's culture. Actually it's team." And I — there are different levels of moats, and this is the ultimate one that determines everything else.

You're saying culture is the ultimate one.

Yes.

But the thing about culture is it's very fragile. There are very few moats I've found that are actually moats. "Moat" is a nice concept. But in reality, you have to replenish your culture.

Ben Horowitz was the speaker in CS183 on Tuesday. I asked him this question about the culture bottleneck in teams.

His book — Hard Things About Hard Things.

Yeah. More concretely — there are so many AI labs today that have all the cash they need, all the compute they need, and they're still not able to ship anything soda. Then you start seeing people leave. My diagnosis is: it's the culture.

So I asked him — Ben's been one of the most aggressive investors in AI labs. He goes back to this thing which resonates a lot. When I used to work at A16Z, I would book a conference room right outside the toilet because it was the fastest way for me to use the bathroom between Zoom meetings.

Sink-by-toilet optimization!

Not healthy in hindsight, but maybe TMI. Outside that conference room on the wall was this quote, printed: "Culture is not a set of beliefs. It's a set of actions." It's by Bushido, a Japanese philosopher.

If you stop taking the actions that demonstrate the mission alignment to what you've said to your team and to the world matters to you, then your culture starts to fray. So it's not actually a moat. It's a very brittle, fragile thing that requires daily tending — like a garden.

But if you figure out the system to keep that garden tended — which ultimately comes down to knowing yourself — because if you're authentic, you'll naturally make trade-offs that seem effortless to you but that reinforce your culture. Then that becomes a very hard thing for other people to catch up to.

Anthropic 的 day-one zeal

At Anthropic, from day one, there was this missionary-like zeal and belief: hey, these capabilities will scale. These systems are stochastic, not deterministic. There will be error bars. And until we crack interpretability, there's risk.

At some point, people will stop using Claude just for coding. They'll use it in some mission-critical context where it'll throw off a bug. And then people are going to come blame them. They want to be on the right side of history — they said, yes, this is a powerful technology, we think it's going to change the world, and we want to be very measured and scientific about the fact that these are statistical models. That's how statistics works.

That belief that safety is important — sometimes it might seem toy-like in the early days, and you could say Anthropic totally over-exaggerated the risk 2 years ago when they said "let's not launch Claude 1." Maybe in hindsight. But hindsight is 20/20. At the time, they didn't know how that model would be used. To them, it felt existential if somebody came and said, "you weren't responsible — this wrote a bug." The liability is massive. How do you prevent against that?
