item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：他在偷偷重建一座"古典美德神殿"
表面上这是一期硅谷 CEO 访谈，但仔细听，Varun 真正在做的事情，是**把雕像重新放回城市广场**。
他做了一个梦，梦里在思考："为什么现代城市不再为'勇气''正义''真理'立雕像了？"——这个细节非常关键。古希腊罗马为抽象美德立神像，是因为他们认为**这些概念需要每天被肉眼提醒**，否则人会忘记。而现代管理学用的全是"OKR""North Star""Mission Statement"——一套去神圣化、去身体化的语言。
Varun 实际上是在做一种**世俗化的宗教重建**：他用"勇气、正义、真理"三座雕像，替代了 KPI 仪表盘。这解释了一个谜：为什么 Clay 的早期决策（按使用量收费、雇过度合格的 content marketer、给 9 个月没起色的员工继续投入）听起来都"不合管理学常识"——因为他不在用管理学决策，他在**用美德伦理学决策**。一家硅谷 SaaS 公司，骨子里跑的是亚里士多德的操作系统。
## 二、跨领域连接：音乐理论 = 产品哲学 = 死亡观
最让我震动的一段，是他把三件看似毫不相

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Story of Lincoln City’s Promotion Miracle | Liam Scully
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：他们把"接班人计划"放上了桌面

整段对话里最让我"啊哈"的瞬间，不是冠军游行，也不是双主帅。而是 Scully 不带任何隐瞒地承认——他们和主教练 Skubala 一起在酒店里讨论"今晚我去见一个可能接你班的人"，主教练的反应是"那哥们我也想见见"。

足球行业的默认操作系统是信息保密 + 情感人质：俱乐部偷偷物色继任者，教练偷偷接听挖角电话，双方互相提防直到爆雷。Lincoln 干脆把这层"地下市场"挪到了地面上。这其实和 Netflix 的"keeper test" 是同构的——"如果你今天来面试，我还会雇你吗？"——把组织里最隐蔽的张力变成共享的、透明的对话。它的真正威力不在于"诚实"这种道德感，而在于：一旦透明化，决策延迟和恐慌成本就归零了。当 Skubala 真的走向 Bristol City 时，Lincoln 不需要从震惊中恢复——他们早就把这次离别在心里彩排过几十遍了。

二、跨领域连接：他们其实在用"工业 OEE"指标管理一支球队

Scully 提了一句被很多人会忽略掉的话——"如果你的工资单都堵在医务室里，那它就帮不了你"，再加上那个不动声色的指标：96% 训练和比赛出勤率。

这套语言不是足球的语言。这是丰田、台积电、半导体晶圆厂的语言——OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)。他们把"运动员"作为一种昂贵的、易损的、必须高利用率运转的生产资本来管理。预算少不是劣势，反而强迫他们去做制造业过去 50 年才学会的事：不是买更多机器，而是把每台机器的可用时间最大化。

更深的洞见在这里：英超大俱乐部因为薪资过剩，实际上从未真正进入制造业的纪律阶段。他们停留在"奢侈品作坊"的运营成熟度。Lincoln 因为穷，被迫提前 20 年进入了下一个范式。当他们升上英冠，他们带着的不是"小俱乐部的怀旧故事"，而是一套操作系统层面的不对称优势。

三、隐藏模式：他们做的每件事，本质上都是"提高观察者的延迟"

把这次对话当作一个系统来看，所有看似无关的决策其实指向同一件事——让对手永远反应不过来：

  • 双主帅 → 没有"那个人离开俱乐部就崩"的单点故障
  • 赛季奖金谈判 → Scully 把它当作"球队心理学的盖革计数器"，对方还以为只是钱的问题
  • 定位球数据 + 6 秒短角球规则 → 在对手分析师还没破解时已经迭代到下一版
  • 驻场创业者 (Entrepreneur in Residence) → 在足球行业还在嘲笑"科技噱头"时，他们已经悄悄在试 30 个失败 + 3 个有效的实验
  • 球场 14 周搞 36 周的工程 → 让供应商和竞争对手都措手不及

这是一种斯多葛式的进攻性。Scully 反复说"我们不反应 (react)，我们回应 (respond)"——这句话直接对应爱比克泰德的"刺激与反应之间有空间"。但他偷偷把它武器化了：只要 Lincoln 的反应空间比对手长一点点，他们就能在每一个细节上获得复利。

四、一个出人意料的悖论：林肯大教堂作为竞争优势

最有趣的细节是把林肯大教堂的视野写进商业计划。一般俱乐部会把"风景好"放在球迷情怀栏。他们把它放进了 B2B 会议宴会业务的定价模型里。这其实和酒店业的"景观房溢价 (view premium)"是同一逻辑——只不过 120 年来从没有英国球场把它做成 SKU。

这暗示了一个更大的真相：英国低级别足球俱乐部坐拥的"地理资产"是这个行业最被低估的资产类别之一。每个老球场都有它的大教堂、它的运河、它的工业遗产景观——但因为这些"不能进球"，从来没人把它纳入资产负债表。Lincoln 在重新定义"足球俱乐部到底拥有什么"。

五、最后一个意外：Liam Scully 真正贩卖的产品不是足球，是"无聊的一致性"

他自己说出了那句魔咒——"我们 boringly consistent 到了让人困倦的程度"。

这是足球行业的反向 USP。所有其他俱乐部都在贩卖戏剧性、英雄主义、救世主。Lincoln 卖的是可预测性。这听起来无聊，直到你意识到——这正是巴菲特和芒格 50 年来贩卖给伯克希尔股东的东西：在一个被肾上腺素驱动的行业里，做那个唯一保持心率稳定的人，复利会替你赢下一切。

最深的讽刺是：那 50,000 人涌上林肯大街的画面，正是"boring consistency"在十年后兑现的、唯一一次允许自己不那么 boring 的瞬间。

📝 转录文本

重逢与开场

Charlie: Liam Scully, round two, welcome back to the Business of Sport.

Liam: Thank you very much for having me back.

Charlie: We have the chance to speak to quite a few executives. You hear the plan and you're like, "Oh, that sounds great," but then actually when it's delivered on, it's pretty special, and you have had the most incredible season.

Liam: Yeah, no, thank you very much for that. And I do think that the proudest thing that we have at Lincoln City Football Club across it all is the fact that many moons ago we sat down, we crafted a plan, we worked our playbook within a plan and, you know, not all of it has gone exactly as you hope or exactly as you expect, but large chunks of it, the key moments, the key pillars of that have largely come together.

So I think as a group of people, from the investors, the owners, the executive — and within, you know, I think it's really nice for us all, the collective, the fact that a lot of what we set out to do we've been able to do. That said, we're not finished yet — this is definitely not the end of the road, but it's definitely a poignant moment and a nice milestone for us to have achieved promotion back to the championship. And when I say back, you know, we've not been at the second tier of English football for 65 years. So yeah, collectively it feels like a really proud achievement.

"Change the game until we find one we can win" — The Top Trumps Philosophy

Charlie: Amazing. There'll probably be two ways we compartmentalize this chat. Firstly, the recognition and celebration that comes from achieving something brilliant for the club. Then there's also, "Right, we've done that — but what's next?"

I loved one comment or quote that was associated — I don't know if you said it, or someone said it about the club — but I saw it and it said: "Change the game until we find one we can win." And it seems like you may have found one that you can win, but the circumstances in which you have won are not the ones that people often anticipate being successful in football.

Liam: No, yeah, thank you for picking that up. And I think, Charlie, the thing for us is — our intention isn't to be anti-establishment, our intention isn't to think that we've found a silver bullet, and we're not trying to go against the grain, and I don't think we actually go against the grain in many places.

I still haven't found a better way to explain this, so I'll give it a go — but do you remember the game Top Trumps as a kid, the cards?

Charlie: Oh yeah, I used to love them.

Liam: What was your favorite type?

Charlie: Yeah, dinosaurs. Dinosaurs and cars.

Liam: Dinosaurs — I think I was dinosaurs and airplanes. Okay, so there we go. But where we looked at this is: if you were able to design your own Top Trump card but you had less than average amount of resource, the way that you would design it in order to compete is — rather than spread your resource over lots of areas — to go, "Right, we want to major in one or two areas. We want to look at what's going to shift the dial, what's going to make the boat go fastest."

And that's really the kind of principles behind everything that we've done at Lincoln City over the years. We understand that there are bigger boys and girls in the playground. We understand that it's a competitive industry. We're not the only club trying to win. We're not the only club trying to do what we're trying to do. But then — let's set about it and go about it our way.

And whilst there is convention, and whilst there are 72, 92, 116 others that are doing it in England whichever way you want to look at it, let's not just do it that way because others are doing it. Let's be really clear with what we're trying to achieve and why we're trying to achieve it. We wanted to focus our resource in certain areas, maximize our outcomes. We probably just asked ourselves why we do things in a certain way for a number of years until we landed on where we felt comfortable.

So we definitely don't think we've got the silver bullet — far from it. We do think we've landed on a methodology that works for Lincoln City, which might not work for others. And we committed — we made a real commitment that if we're going to do something then we're going to do it, you know, two-footed, all in, absolutely go for it rather than being pensive.

And I do think, being quite blunt: when you don't have as much resource, when you don't have the option, I think it can be quite liberating, and we've embraced that.

The Liberation of Constraints

Charlie: You mean by that you're not tempted necessarily to do things because you just can't? I.e., let's be honest, when we're talking about this really, it's often the money side of it, right? If you don't have the financial resource you have to find other ways.

Liam: Yeah, certain things that have tested us along the way might be decisions in other clubs, but when you don't have it, you can't spend it. Effectively that's — we've been able to be really steadfast in that. And equally, where we have had resource and where we've built resource, where we've self-developed, where the owners have supported us, the jeopardy of ensuring that we were really efficient with that has always been important.

So we were very comfortable with who we are and what we've done. We don't think it would work for everybody, and we're not trying to be far too cocky and think we've achieved anything outside of what is for Lincoln City.

Charlie: Humility is a beautiful thing in sport, and it's actually quite rare, so it's nice to hear. I don't want you to compromise that, but I do certainly want to recognize this achievement, because what I like about this is it shows that you can achieve success without spending a huge amount of money. We know the problems — last time you were here, you said something really interesting around the dangers and threats to football: the whims of the people with a huge amount of money that prop up clubs. You said, "Look, we're only six people walking away realizing that this is not the best place for them to be spending their money from a disaster happening within football."

What you've shown and delivered is that the thing everyone assumes — that spending money will give you the best chance of success — doesn't have to be the case. Correct me if I'm wrong, but you came into this season with a bottom-third in the league budget. And I've sat in this chair having chats with people where they go, "Yeah, generally in League One and below, the size of your budget correlates to the position you have in the league table." So where did you ensure that you had the discipline to not lose your edge?

Building the System: People, Robustness, and the End of the "Unicorn Manager"

Liam: The first thing that we looked at is ultimately: how do you construct a team and how do you build something that still has the edge and is still really laser-focused on achieving results and outcomes.

First of all, we invested into people — off-field staff, technical team. For the size of our operation, the number of staff, the quality of staff within the training ground, the EPC (the Elite Performance Center), that was where we very deliberately invested. It is a talent-based industry, and of course we have to look at the players — ultimately they're going to be the ones that score the goals and do the work on the field. But if we can resource around them and ensure that our elite athletes are given the absolute best chance to succeed, that's something that we were going to do.

The other thing that we wanted to move away from is this theory that the gaffer, the manager — you have this unicorn single individual at the football club that is a single point of success or failure for everything that goes on in and around that green rectangle. We really wanted to move away from that. It wasn't us trying to reinvent football, it was just around the fact that if it goes really well and then that person leaves you — which might be topical — it can't then tumble down like a deck of cards.

Part of what we tried to build is systems and processes and infrastructure, and then continuously make decisions at the point of most knowledge. A really quick example would be: if we're going to have a small but talented squad, we have to ensure that we have robustness. We have to ensure that our players are fit and available. From a pure sports science and medical point of view, we had very few soft tissue injuries last year, which meant that as much of the time we were able to get our players training and out on the pitch and contributing.

Now, that had to be a commitment right from the player ID and recruitment stages, right through to coaching and methodology. How that worked on a day-to-day basis — probably too much detail to go into here — but a commitment was: we want 96% availability on training and matches for our players. And then we had to build systems, processes, infrastructure that would go and support that, which then allows us to have a small but talented squad — we're not having to have 25, 26, 27 players.

So it's examples like that that we went, "Right, let's do this and let's commit." Quite bluntly put: if you have your payroll tied up in the medical room, that's not helping you.

Charlie: Yeah, exactly — it's a problem.

Holes in the Boat: Coach Succession and Transparent Planning

Charlie: You mentioned there that you need to do what happens to make the boat go faster — whatever it is. In football there are also a lot of things that can put a hole in the boat. Overreliance on particular things, as you just said, is a key part of it. Michael Skubala is now no longer with you — that was the point I think you were referencing when you said "topical, live example." Managers come and go. He achieved brilliant success with you, now gone to Bristol City. You've moved quickly, and the solution that you've found is also really interesting.

Liam: Charlie, there's certainly layers to this. The first thing we probably ought to talk about is our succession planning and the transparency within that. When we recruited Michael many moons ago now, and we started building systems and processes and working with him, what we were also really transparent with him about is that we're constantly going to be looking for the next head coach of Lincoln City.

This is probably where people assume that this might be taboo, but we were talking to Michael about that process going on. It was always predicated by the fact that he leaves through the front door, it was always predicated by the fact that this is going to be successful and we want to help you in your career, and if you go faster than us then we have to accept that there'll be a stepping-off point for you.

It wasn't something that was clandestine, it wasn't something that we shied away from. Every now and then — once or twice in a year — myself, our sporting director, and Michael, we would be very open with those conversations.

A little anecdote: there was even one point at a hotel during an away game where our sporting director told Michael, "Look, I've got a meeting this evening — it's actually with someone who, you know, who knows what that role could be." And Michael's going, "Oh brilliant, I'd love to actually meet him — I've heard really good things about him."

So I think when you have authenticity, transparency, people feel — I don't want to sound corny — but psychologically safe in their role. Then these things can happen above the wall line.

Charlie: Jumping in there — does that come both ways then? Does that create that relationship of just absolute comfort between you, where if there's something that he gets approached for, he feels equally as comfortable giving you the heads-up?

Liam: Yeah, absolutely. And I guess the reason why we were able to react and do what we have had to do at Lincoln City with that change is because equally he was transparent, he was open with us, so it wasn't really a surprise.

I just think that there is a lot of work that you have to do when the moment happens. Our job as executives in football — whichever style works, you know what I've just outlined there might not work for some people and they might think that's a little too extreme — but our job is to be prepared for all eventualities. The number one thing we had to do is always be prepared for what happens next.

When you're able to build relationships and things aren't a surprise, that's when you're able to do some of your best work. Of course, I'm not naive to some of the sensitivities around that. First of all, in order to go and do that you have to build that bone-deep trust as a group of human beings that work together. But I think when you behave like we behaved at Lincoln City — this is going into my 10th season, we've fired one head coach only — we're not a club that hires and fires.

What you're able to do is show that the way that we'll act in the future is based on the evidence of the way we've worked in the past, and I think that gives people comfort. So I don't think this is any one thing that we've done in one moment — I think it is just years of actually being boringly consistent.

Charlie: It's working.

Liam: And that enables you to do things. We never react — we respond. I think that's a really great way of thinking about it: in our industry we have other teams that are trying to beat us, we have other things going on. If we react emotionally or do things knee-jerk, then we'll get that wrong. So we have to respond, but we have to be methodical and logical in our approach.

The Dual Head Coach Solution

Charlie: And your solution to Michael leaving has been pretty standout as well. Tell us.

Liam: We've always believed we invest in people. We have two absolute outstanding practitioners in Chris Cohen and Tom Shaw working with Michael Skubala. We have long been developing the systems and process, what day-to-day looks like at the training ground.

I absolutely don't want to take anything away from Michael as our head coach — he definitely was a huge part of our success. But equally, what I've got to say is he wasn't the success — it wasn't just down to him. There was a large number of people that contributed to that at the training ground and across the whole club.

In our evaluation around when we thought this was happening — "What do we need to do next?" — the biggest thing we felt we would want is continuity. We felt that with the group we've got assembled, with where we are in our cycle, continuity was a part of that. But equally, we're not romantic — we tested that with external applicants, we tested what could this look like if it's not the same. Is it one point of difference, ten points of difference?

Ultimately the question that we continually asked ourselves is: "What is best for Lincoln City right now?" And we do have outstanding people. The belief isn't just about doing the right thing for them as human beings — that is part of it — it's actually part of what we've designed: what day-to-day looks like, the amount of disruption or lack of disruption we believe we can have going forward.

I'll labor it one more — we definitely didn't go, "How can we do something a bit weird and a bit quirky?" That wasn't the intention. But when we felt it was the right solution, we equally didn't sit there going, "No, we can't do that because nobody else does it." That's a key differential for us.

Charlie: Two head coaches you can look at as having lots of benefits — different opinions, perspectives, approaches, with a very balanced mutual respect between the two, otherwise you wouldn't have ever made that decision. But sometimes you need a point of clarity. Football's such a difficult environment from a leadership perspective.

Liam: Absolutely. Let's be really clear: are there some things in this system that we've set up that are not optimal? Yes, of course. But we're looking at the net, the total effect of what we have — do the benefits outweigh the negatives?

Based on where we are in our cycle, what we have in the building, what we've seen every single day for the last two years in building to where we've got to — we believe the net best choice for Lincoln City is doing this.

Everybody goes, "Okay, who makes the decision and the substitution? Who's going to sign the player?" Our response is: we are systems and processes. As boring as this may sound, a substitution is scenario-based. A lot of that work goes on Monday to Friday. The theory that we can sit and watch a game on a Saturday and just make decisions — a lot of the work has to go in. There's data that sits behind it, there's planning, preparation, analysis. So a lot of what goes on a Saturday between 3:00 and 5:00 PM is scenario-planned Monday to Friday.

Of course there are going to be moments in games where you have to feel it, where you have to make decisions that are sporting. That's something that Chris and Tom have worked through. They were in charge of our in-game strategy last year. The only difference is that they had somebody that they had to sell their recommendation to in order to rubber-stamp that decision. So in some ways not a lot has changed, in some ways a lot has changed.

But it goes back to: this works for Lincoln City, with Chris Cohen and with Tom Shaw, and with where we are in our cycle. That's the only reason why we're doing this — not because we think this is going to be the new way going forward.

The Season's Story: From Preseason Mood to "It's On"

Charlie: For five to ten minutes, indulge us in the romanticism. Just put down those processes and tell us about what it was actually like last season. It would have been an incredible story if you'd mapped that through going, "Oh wow, we're doing quite well. Oh, maybe we're punching a bit above where we are. We could go up. Oh, we could actually win the league."

Liam: There was a really interesting dynamic very early on in preseason that probably surprised me. Attributes that we'd lost from player sales — record player sales — the narrative around that was it's going to be really difficult to replace. What we were able to do is the board were brilliant and they allowed us to reinvest a large amount of that. So we actually felt coming out of the window, despite losing a few of the key names, we had a more balanced scorecard.

Every chief executive or sporting director in football will tell you that in preseason there is a little game that goes on with the leadership group and the players: the squad bonus. The squad bonus is an annually negotiated financial reward, and every club has a slightly different structure. Effectively, depending on where the team are based, depending on how many points are on the board, that's the group bonus at that moment in time.

The truth is, as a chief exec, you probably know your group's in trouble or that you've not got the recruitment right if the team captain and the leadership group are sat arguing with you as to what the bonus points system looks like for 15th place. Because at that point you're going, "Oh, I'm not sure they back themselves."

The conversations we had with our leadership group was about automatic promotion and top six. I left those meetings going, "Oh, I tell you what — that's interesting." The psychology here — knowing we had I think at the time the 18th place budget — that group quietly and with humility backed themselves. What they were negotiating with the chief exec wasn't 15th place — it was what it looks like if we're in the top six, what it looks like if we're automatically promoted. That was a great litmus test, a great mood-feeler.

As the season went on, momentum built. There was a really interesting moment coming out of January when we were hovering around it, and the whole mood of the training ground wasn't "Can we stay in there?" — the mood was we're chasing Cardiff who were in first. I remember thinking: "My job's just to get out of the way here."

Charlie: At what point did you start to genuinely think, "We can do this — and by do this I mean go up automatically, not even playoff."

Liam: There were several moments along the way. We played Cardiff away. In no way, shape, or form did it look like a League One game. Massive amount of respect for the people at Cardiff — Brian Barry-Murphy's assembled a really good football team there. It was a contrast of styles: a very technically based team, big new stadium, ex-Championship coming down. Little Lincoln City, a little bit more direct, a little bit more heavy-metal football. It was completely contrasting in its styles. We went to their home — they were brilliant — and if I'm honest, without sounding a little bit arrogant, we were just better on the day and executed better, and we walked away with a 2-0 victory. 3,000 Lincoln City fans away in the Welsh Capital having a great time. It was just like, "Oh okay — this is now on. This is really serious."

That goes back to knowing when to get out of the way and just allowing the momentum to take it forward.

The Bank of Goodwill: Fans, Asset Sales, and Consistency

Charlie: It must be a lovely thing in football when it's going well and you have a season like you had — the feeling that must have been around that stadium as the belief increased.

Liam: The building blocks came from right in the very beginning of the season when we lost our captain — we sold our jewel in the crown, Jovon Makama, to Norwich City, record sale for the club. Most clubs probably at that time might be getting petrol bombs through the letterbox. But our fans were brilliant.

I do think that "boringly consistent" helps, because there is evidence-based to what we've done before, and what we say we're going to do, we're going to do. We talked about: we have to trade our way up through the pyramid, we said that there'll be times and points we have to walk away from deals because we can't afford it. And they understood.

The mood around the fan base and in the stadium — whether we try and deny it or not, the environment within a stadium does help or hinder performance. The mood of the fan base ultimately turbocharged and became a huge part of the performance sphere. On day one of the season, when we sold one of our star assets and it becomes public three hours before kickoff, their mood was brilliant. They responded phenomenally well. They doubled down and it was like: "No no, we're us, we'll continue being us."

I hope that's because of years and years of us being consistent, doing what we said we would do, and always having their interest in mind. When we talk about "we've done this together" — I don't mean in the business, I mean the City of Lincoln, the County of Lincolnshire, the fan base — they've been a huge part of it, and they help set the tone as well.

Ownership: Commitment Even When They Disagree

Charlie: This doesn't happen without excellent ownership. You've had a slight shift in the ownership — Ron Fowler's taken a bigger control role this year. How has that relationship developed?

Liam: I genuinely think we're lucky to have the best owners in football, and that goes from Clive Nates, to the Jabara family, and to Ron Fowler now. They've all had a universal trait.

The first thing they do is they work with us to set a strategic plan. They give us the space and the bandwidth to then go and deliver that. There's never a message at 5:00 PM after a bad result. Everything's done at a macro level, not micro level, when they analyze our progress. Going back to "react or respond" — what it's enabled us to do within the executive team is they knew we would have to be systematic and structured to build something. So they've given us the space and bandwidth to do that.

There's been huge check and challenge, huge debate — but once we sign something off and commit, they commit. And in particular the most impressive thing I have from our ownership is they commit when they disagree with us. So when we leave the room and we said, "This is the way we are going to do it," even if they go, "Chief Exec, I think you're a little bit crazy" — you wouldn't know that. Nobody would know that. They absolutely back us in order to go and deliver.

If that's putting their name to it in an interview or whatever it may be, they would give us that support and backing. They deserve a huge amount of credit not just for writing the checks — of course they have supported us financially — but how they've done that and how they challenge us is really important.

Ron Fowler's exactly the same. Let's be really honest — he's been the controlling owner of the San Diego Padres. Lincoln City versus San Diego Padres — we're probably half a department compared to the size and scale. But there's been no suggestion that the Padres know how to do it better. There's been no suggestion that "this is how it is." It's always about, "Let me try these ideas, let me challenge you here" — knowing what they know, knowing what they don't know, but always with absolute check and challenge.

Charlie: I get asked a lot — American ownership: good or bad? Well, depends — if you're a Chelsea fan you have a different perspective to an Arsenal fan right now. You can't bucket it all in the same place. But when it does go right and they do come in with a level of experience, expertise, perspective that we don't have here, it can be excellent. Where has he leaned in where he's been particularly valuable?

Liam: First of all, he really challenged us to think bigger at times. We're currently doing 36 weeks' worth of building work at the stadium in 14 weeks in the close season.

Charlie: Makes sense.

Liam: Which is good fun.

Charlie: Especially with time and the builders.

Liam: Absolutely. But when we iterated, I remember the first presentation of what we're doing, and it was almost like, "Nah. Be bigger than that. Try harder." There was probably three or four attempts where we're going, "Okay, is this big enough an idea for you next?" And he goes, "Na na na." Whilst it was playful and thought it really set the tone, it's like: "No no, this is the vision. This is what it looks like. I will back you to this level, we'll back the business plan, but let's look at where the waterline is."

Of course that needs financing in order to support. But everything we're doing is based on a return on investment, and there is a real solid business plan behind that. There's not one ounce of this that is a vanity project. That's where Ron has really shifted the dial for us — our ambitions and some of what we're looking at have been really challenged to be even more creative. Sometimes we have to row back from that. He goes: "Okay, now you've ran off the edge of the pier — I didn't mean that, that's too far."

What it's also enabled us to do and challenged us to do is take some risks and go, "Okay, once we sign it off, I'm not going to be on your head be it. We're in it collectively." That's the true spirit of the club, and that helps massively.

Stadium: 14 Weeks to Reinvent Sincil Bank

Charlie: So stadiums are hallowed turf for fans — they're also now key drivers of improved business and opportunity. In your 14 weeks, what are you doing to elevate that experience?

Liam: We love our home. Sincil Bank has been the home of Lincoln City for 120 years. It is a phenomenal place for football — it's an amphitheater. For the 95 minutes of a game it is just a phenomenal place to be. The hair stands up on the back of your neck.

But actually outside of a match day — well, broader than that on the match day, but then outside of a match day — it is a really difficult property to generate revenue from. So everything we're looking to do here is: how do we enhance the match day experience? How do we give our fans the best possible experience? How do we drive value? And how do we take a property of 120 years of age, built over various different iterations, different architects, different designs, different eras, and do that in a way that helps generate revenue outside of the match day?

One of the things we believe is that we've got the best view in football. We have the phenomenal Lincoln Cathedral — our stadium sits, and if you look into the horizon, it's just a beautiful view of one of the most beautiful buildings in England. So our new hospitality suites: we've ripped away our executive boxes, we've created new lounges, we're going to have the only conference and banqueting space in Lincolnshire for 500 people plus. It will work on a match day, and it will work on a non-match day.

I'm not sure anyone's ever tried to do it in 14 weeks, but that's part of the fun of it.

Charlie: Change the game.

Liam: Yeah, exactly. Change the game until we find one we can win. That's the mentality and the approach. Lots of other football clubs will be doing this, so we're not unique. But what you can do in Lincoln versus, you know — I won't name them — it has to be based on Lincoln. We have Lincoln Cathedral. Let's lean into that. Let's make the most of that. We're just not a copy and paste of any other football club. We have to find the best solution for Lincoln City.

From League One to Championship: The Financial Leap

Charlie: How's the financial health of the club today from where you would want it to be? Looking at profit and loss simply, but then also the revenue streams — commercial, match day, venue, merch.

Liam: As we leave League One, which we spent seven years in and we got to know every corner of that division. Despite having bigger boys and girls in the playground, we were good performers in all those aspects — commerciality, match day revenue — it was always above par for where a club our size would be.

The big difference clearly going into the championship is eyeballs. I'm a huge fan of the Premier League, and the Premier League is the best league in world football. But what we've also not to forget is the championship is the sixth or seventh best league in world football. And in England we're just really spoiled by the fact that we have the Premier League. The Premier League's a global league. But we are going into one of the top leagues in the world for interest, for eyeballs, for size of stadiums, for quality of the game.

So the biggest change for us is the broadcast revenue and the amount of times we're on live TV and the amount of attention we're going to get. We have to lean into that. When you look at the revenue and say, "Oh yeah, we've grown by 70-80% in that change," the reality is the broadcast deal has done that. What we've got to do is use the opportunity to maximize everything else that goes around that.

We're investing in new technology within the stadium to maximize that. We're restructuring some of our commercial deals, our retail partnership, and various other things — because what works in a League One landscape doesn't work in a Championship landscape. It's not just the playbook spilled up — it's actually largely a very different playbook, not least because we have very different financial rules between one division and the other.

We don't go, "Well, we think we did all right in the League One setting, therefore we just rinse-repeat just bigger in the Championship." A lot of what we've been doing is understanding what works and what doesn't within that Championship setting. There are key cornerstones — commercial revenue, kit property, advertising boards — but there are lots of new opportunities available to us as well.

Charlie: Just to be clear, the difference in broadcast revenue can be about £10 million right into the championship. So you're suddenly dealing with a really huge amount of money when turnover could have been around — let's say between 10 to 15 previously in League One. You're almost 100% on that side. But what people miss is then the costs. So the discipline you talked about at the beginning, the processes in place to not slip into the mentality that allows you to go and lose a huge amount of money, OR be overly dependent on revenue that is far from secure beyond now — is probably the most important job you have.

Liam: The skill element within this is calibrating the appetite for risk. If we don't take risks, we will be relegated and we'll be back to where we started. If we take the safety catch off and go gung-ho, we might stay up, we might be relegated — and if we get relegated having taken the safety catch off, boy, are we in trouble. Two ends of the spectrum where neither outcome is particularly reliable.

So we have to calibrate our attitude and appetite for risk — in terms of the contracts we offer, how we structure things, how many people we bring into the off-field team from a commercial point of view. That's been the hardest pure technical skill element we've had to calibrate over the last few months, because it is subjective and strongly opinion-based.

Charlie: How much of that is you taking that risk versus you and Ron versus Ron taking a risk? Your job is to deliver based on the financials available — his job is to decide what financials are available.

Liam: That's been a very collaborative process. We've spoken about that at great length. We wanted to make sure we went through understanding, alignment, commitment. We are in a committed stage now — the business plan has commitment from ownership in a best-case and worst-case scenario.

We have alignment, because when we worked through the process, it wasn't just a chief exec banging a budget on the table going, "This is the one plan and if it's not this it's not going to work." Along the journey we didn't just say what — we said why — and we worked in the understanding phase. So when we were designing the business plan over the next two to three years, we spent the large majority of our time working with Ron, with the other investors, in the understanding phase. "If this, then that." That enabled us to spend probably 80% of our time in that design phase, and then once there was a deep enough understanding for what we were looking to achieve, that's when we cycled through and put all the building blocks in place.

The attitude to risk and the financing of risk was of course signed off by Ron and Andrew Fowler, and ultimately liquid investments — our ultimate owners. But it was iterative. It wasn't AB, it wasn't binary. We worked through it. We have a process that will work for Lincoln City, a business plan that will work for Lincoln City — that will give us the best possible chance to survive and hopefully one day thrive within the championship. But equally we've not put the farm on red or black. It wouldn't be ideal if we go back down, but it won't be catastrophic.

Regulation, Squad Cost, and the New Era of Football Governance

Charlie: Squad cost, new regulations, football regulator — we've had a lot of change in the management of football in this country. You've been on the EFL board, you're running a club, now running a club in a brand new environment. What are the biggest changes and how is this impacting your day-to-day job?

Liam: You're absolutely right — we work in a regulated industry now. That's the first time that has happened. Net overall, it's a good thing — I firmly believe that — but there are challenges that come with that.

The amount of money we're going to have to spend on governance, the amount of investment we're going to have. On one hand, you can say that's a good thing — scenario planning, worst-case scenarios, evidence we have to submit that we've got contingency plans. Definitely there's an argument for "this is a good thing." But there is a lot of work having to go into: what does the total wipeout situation look like? What are you mitigating against?

Big businesses, of course, will have this. But sometimes we need to not forget that some of these clubs are League Two clubs — revenues of three or four million versus top of the championship, huge 40, 50, 60 million revenue clubs. There are massive differences in scale within that, so the regulator has to be proportionate with its application.

The regulator is a game changer for the industry — truthfully some good, some bad — but it's here. So we roll our sleeves up, we get on with it, we accept, and it's our job to work within the parameters it gives. Net overall positive — but good governance and ensuring we have good governance is now a critical responsibility of the chief executive.

Communication, Trust, and the "Bank of Goodwill"

Charlie: I was kind of looking through this — I looked at promotion, change of managers, change of ownership, stadium development — it was like, "Whoa, this is a lot in a short space of time." How has it been balancing all of this in a relatively short space of time?

Liam: Unfiltered truth is: going 29 games unbeaten, 103 points — at that point the fans are clearly going to be supportive and backing and understanding. The hard work that goes in actually is when the optics maybe aren't as good. We always call it the "bank of goodwill" — you have to deposit into that bank of goodwill when maybe the on-field and the things the fans are there for (which is winning football) aren't going well.

So what I'd like to think we've done at Lincoln City over the last decade is the fact that we've always been visible, we've always communicated transparently — in particular when times aren't going so well or we're not quite hitting the sweet spot. So much so, the head coach that has captained the ship for those 29 games unbeaten, for those 103 points — when that person moves on (and rightly so, and we wish him well in his career) and "what comes next" happens, the fans so far have given us their approval for the decisions we made, our rationale, and why we've done it.

There's probably no bigger test of confidence and trust in the club than when we do something a little quirky after the season that we've just had. But I don't think that's because of the last 10 minutes — I think that's because of the last 10 years. Hopefully what the fans do, and they reciprocate, is they almost unconditionally back our judgment at that moment in time.

What we said we'd do is really important to us — and that's in particular at times of giving bad news. At least let's be transparent and communicate that rather than hiding away.

The Bus Parade: An Unforgettable Moment

Charlie: You said it was a pretty special moment to share with them when you did win the league and you had the celebrations in the city.

Liam: That was — I'll never forget that moment in my life. That was incredible. The city bus parade — people won't know the Lincoln geography, but we're sat on the open-top bus outside just outside the back of the stadium, and we're there for 20 minutes waiting for the official parade time. And if I'm honest, I'm looking down the street thinking: "I don't think anyone's going to turn up to this. I can hardly see anyone. I think this is going to be the most embarrassing moment in football history."

Then we got to the roundabout and turned the corner, and the High Street in Lincoln — 40 to 50,000 people came out within the city. Bear in mind the population's 100,000 within the City of Lincoln. It was just incredible. But I don't think the scale was the most impressive thing — because the High Street in Lincoln, and the bus could hardly move, we spent a lot of time just stuck. You were able to look people in the eyes and see what it meant to them and the passion and the care and the support.

I'm a bit of an emotional void at times, but that was a real lump in the throat, and you're like: "Oh my God, this is special." That was a really cool moment because the players, the staff, everybody that had been involved in it, was there to witness it.

One of the things we really wanted is the players' families on the buses — we had an extra bus on because we wanted the players to experience this with their families as well. The people that have sacrificed the most over the last 12 months and prior to that are their families. So for them to be able to experience it I think was really really important for us. That was a really cool moment.

Charlie: It's probably a funny moment for you as a CEO. People probably don't give enough recognition to executives in football, where you do have to separate emotion from outcome quite often. To have an experience like that — actually, that is just a moment, that's a day to not worry about what's happening next, to not think about something that's gone wrong in the past, to let yourself enjoy that moment. Because you, as much as the players, as much as the fans, are part of the team that allow that to happen. But you probably very rarely let yourself even recognize that.

Liam: No, the — my wife's brilliant and she supports me unconditionally. The last thing she said to me as I was leaving the house — because I was winging about a few things — "My name's on the safety certificate, I'm a bit grumpy about X, Y, and Z, oh this person wants this" — she went, "Oh, will you just please enjoy this." In fairness, she rarely gives me advice, but when she does, I do listen. And yeah, it was a great moment.

But broader than that — and I genuinely mean this — this isn't meant to be condescending — it's taken a village for Lincoln City to have the success we have. Behind the scenes, and to be able to share that moment with so many people and to see it — that was really special. There were two bits: looking down in the fans' eyes and seeing what it meant to them, and then looking back in the eyes of the people on the bus seeing what that meant to them. They were really cool moments.

I remember turning around to our head of medical, who used to be a scholar at the club. He went away and did his medical qualifications, then was our Academy physiotherapist, and then became our head — and he's from Lincoln. I remember turning around going, "Oh mate, this is special" — and looking in his eyes and him looking back at me too. I think those are the bits in football that make it all the more worthwhile.

From Canoe to Cruise Ship — But Still Punching Up

Charlie: Previously you said to me you're up against people who are in a nuclear sub, and you're in a canoe. Can we move you out of a canoe? Where are we at? Sunseeker? Small cruise ship / ferry?

Liam: Cruise ship. No, I think we've definitely got a motor on our boat now.

Charlie: So the paddles are gone.

Liam: Yeah, we can let go of the paddles. But I'm not too sure it's much bigger than that. Going back to Charlie, it can be quite liberating knowing who you are, what you've got, and where we are.

We know how hard the challenge is going to be going into one of the top leagues in the world next year. League One in the UEFA coefficient is about the 37th league, Championship is about the 7th league. We've not jumped up one league — we've jumped up 30 leagues. So we are definitely not approaching this naive. We're definitely not sat here thinking we've got the answers to everything — we definitely don't. But equally, there is part of us that goes, "Okay, as long as we're us, and as long as we continue being us and commit to being us, we believe that that will be good enough." And you have to enjoy it.

Charlie: So many times this season I was just looking at that Championship table. It is incredible. How many massive teams are playing in that league? And now you, Bolton, Cardiff adding to that. There are games every single weekend that you and the fans will get to enjoy. That's not void of expectation, that's not void of you performing well. But it is a privilege, right? You've slugged away for seven years where, if we're being honest, probably a lot of people would have expected you to be down the bottom rather than at the top, and you've made that jump.

Liam: I remember a fan at one of the local fan forums — he kept saying, "Oh Liam, you keep telling us that we want to look up the pyramid, not down. But show us what you mean, tell us what you mean by that."

I think we've got to be really careful — if you stand in a building full of people, and as a genuine leader you say, "Look, we've got this shot put and we're going to hit the moon," they'll look at you and go, "Okay, come on." But the big thing I'd say is what I've learned is: you definitely do not put a ceiling on a group of people. You definitely do not cap what is possible.

So there's not one ounce of what we're doing going into next year that is intending to be tourists. We are a serious professional outfit. We do things our way, and we're there to compete. We don't want to have the mentality of "Oh, if we get beat, so what" — we definitely don't have that. We just have to get that calibration between realism and expectation.

A quick example: teams that stay up often average just over 52 points. The average promoted team wins three away games in their first season.

Charlie: Really?

Liam: Three away games. That's 20-odd away games that you're coming away very empty-handed.

Charlie: 1/5th of the points — that's fascinating.

Liam: So how can we attack that and do that differently? What if we can be the club that goes and wins five or six? Everybody says home form's really important — for example. So whilst there is balanced and measured expectation, equally there is a little bit of steel behind the scenes of: "You know what? We're going to be us, we're going to take it our way, and we believe it will be enough."

The biggest challenge you'll always have as a leader is calibrating that — that it's not blind arrogance or naivety, but equally you're not denting aspiration and putting a ceiling on a group.

Recruitment in a New Universe: From League of Ireland to the Bernabéu

Charlie: How different is operating in the transfer market — or recruitment environment — in a Championship compared to a League One?

Liam: I'll tell this anecdote, and I don't mean it condescendingly. The way our processes work — when it gets to the end-point with clips and videos, towards the back end of last season when we were looking at recruitment — with all due respect, when we're watching clips of players, we're used to watching players running around maybe in a team in the League of Ireland or maybe a League Two team or League One team within England.

And this clip comes on the screen — and it's the Bernabéu.

I'm going, "Is this — who are we after here?"

Charlie: (laughing) We're shopping in Madrid now.

Liam: Look, it was a qualifier game. But that was definitely an interesting moment where we looked at each other and went, "Ah, the game's changed here, hasn't it."

Where I think we can thrive as Lincoln City — the unfiltered truth is the Premier League and the Championship is a really interesting proposition for players across the world. They know that English football has the best leagues, so they want to come to the Championship. We are one of the few Championship football clubs and few Premier League football clubs — we're in that family now. Our opportunity to go and shop in that global market has come now. If we want to be precious about this — that's not because we're Lincoln City, that's because we are in one of the top seven leagues of this world. So that has opened up a brand new market for us.

What we've got to do is choreograph: what does that relationship and that journey look like for that individual, for that human being? Because for the last seven years we've talked about: come to Lincoln City, join us, and either you go to the Championship because you go faster than us, or we go there together. Whereas now we're hopefully talking about: come to us, and you go to the Premier League, or you go with [us]. So that does take a recalibration, because the type of player, quite frankly the quality of player, the history of where they've come from, is very different.

However, what we've also got to be really respectful of is we've got an incredible group of people — some of them pre-peak in their development curve, some of them quite frankly post-peak — that have come and done an unbelievable thing at Lincoln City. So we've got to get the balance right between respecting what's gone on in the past and what's going on in the future. Our build is going to be sensitive and respectful of that — again, not romantic — but equally never put a ceiling on a group of players.

Charlie: It's a fine balance, isn't it? You are one of the most humble people in football that I've ever met, I promise you. And I'd imagine that is a quality that has transitioned throughout the organization. At the same time, football is also about strength and confidence, and some form of overt performance of self-belief, to encourage people in that situation that this is the place you have to go to. So for you, it is a fine balance between having that internally and then being able to turn on something when the time is right for a conversation where you need to sell a bit of a dream.

Liam: That's where a lot of what we've done in the past still translates. Being really honest, our best recruitment tool is players that have left us for a range of reasons. One of the things I'm really proud about is the way our players talk about us after they've moved on. Sometimes that's successful stories and sometimes that's not — I'm not going to name them individually, it'd be unfair the scenarios — but we have definitely signed players in the last three, four, five years purely and simply because of feedback, referrals, whatever you want to call it from colleagues, either players that they're playing with currently or players they've played with in the past.

Because they talk about what the environment's like at Lincoln City, the level of support you get. Now, this isn't woolly fluffy — this is still elite sport, this isn't "we're all sat on bean bags." Don't mistake our kindness for weakness. There is a real edge to what we do. But equally, there is a way that you can go about things — and if you put the human being at the center of that, very rarely do you have a misalignment.

On Liam's Own Strengths and Weaknesses

Charlie: Out of interest — you've done this now for a while — what part of the job comes most naturally to you, and what part have you had to maybe work on a little bit?

Liam: The bit that I really enjoy is people and solving problems. The best thing in the seat that I'm in is that you do have the ability to make decisions that ultimately can impact people's lives. That's the coolest thing with the job. You have to act responsibly with that, but looking someone in the eye and backing them and believing in them — that's not just on-field talent.

One of the proudest things we have at Lincoln City is our alumni — people that have come in, worked for the organization, that have then gone on to work at the very top of sports business. I think it's one of the coolest things. Meeting up with them every now and then: "How's life?" Speaking to a couple of our former colleagues that have just come back from the Monaco Grand Prix working at McLaren F1 — you go, "That's pretty cool. I remember you as an intern" or "I remember you coming in fresh out of university in an entry-level job." Those are some of the coolest stories we have, and they become our best salespeople and recruitment people.

If I have one talent in this world, I do think it's working with people and bringing a team along and creating an environment that is both fun but equally is high performance.

To answer the other side: as a chief exec, ultimately responsible — you have things from a health and safety, governance, regulation point of view — I can't lie and say I get ecstatic about reading our latest safety report. But you have to operate with balance. One of the things working through a lower league club — you have to be ambidextrous. You have to have the ability to do as many things as possible, because without that, you don't have balance within the organization.

But football's a wonderful industry with wonderful people. The thing maybe the fans don't see is it's really collegiate. We'd all kick our granny on a Saturday for three points — there is no doubt that we're competitive beasts — but I promise you, maybe because it's the pressure of the industry, Monday to Friday it's really collegiate. There are some proper top human beings that will always go out their way to help you and work with you. That's pretty cool within the industry.

Efficiency, Set Pieces, and an AI Edge

Charlie: A couple of random questions. Firstly, 21st Group listed Lincoln as the most sustainable and efficient club in England. What does it mean to be the most sustainable and efficient club?

Liam: "Efficient" is everything we drive. The board measure me and us on cost per point. They measure us on over-performance — that is the golden thread and expectation. For somebody as credible as the 21st Group to run their analysis and say we are the most efficient over a prolonged period of time — that's really nice. Lots of people have contributed to that: systems, processes. But what we can't do is sit here and celebrate that and go, "Well, we've got it." The challenge is going to be how do we do that at the next level.

I look at our business plan, I look at the things we have in place: we will be promoted with the smallest loss probably in the last decade. We will lose two million pounds in the year that we got promoted. There are certain teams that lost 35 million, 50 million going up. We've got to go and do the same thing at the next level. Efficiency and effectiveness are probably the two buzzwords we'd want around Lincoln City.

Charlie: That's a serious achievement — that's incredible. I hear some shocking numbers here. One of my favorite media quotes was: "Lincoln City is English football's model club, and the other clubs should learn from them — but they probably won't."

The other bit — I think you're the highest-scoring side in England from set pieces? And part of the reason was the use of an AI tool? In my head it's just Set Piece FC — Arsenal — dominating that, but you've even usurped them.

Liam: It goes back to what makes our boat go the fastest. Several years ago, our sporting director and the team thought, "How do we be really effective?" In football a number of years ago, because Pep did it, it became fashionable: how to restart from a goal kick. 20 years ago it was how far can you punt it, and then all of a sudden you've got center-backs rolling in and your six dropping down. Restarts became the thing.

We just wanted to take that to the next level — effectiveness in terms of restarts from throws, from set pieces. We just felt we could weaponize that. It goes back to "How do we be the absolute best?" Looking and embracing innovation: we worked with a company to analyze tens of millions of corners, tens of millions of throws, and understand how we could best do it.

Now, I'm not going to give away too much. But for example: short corners, quick short corners give you the best probability. But defining "quick" is under six seconds. More than six seconds, then it has the least probability. Delivering the ball into a certain area of the box...

Charlie: Interesting.

Liam: What it needed is skilled practitioners to translate that data. And it would need a culture and environment within the training ground to really embrace and commit to wanting to do that. There aren't many footballers that love doing set-piece training at the end of training, and the repetition. So that required whole-club buy-in from the players, the staff. It wasn't just the intelligence — it was about how you apply it.

Set pieces have become vogue now, and there are going to be lots of teams that then go and replicate. That's what happens with the industry — it constantly evolves. Once somebody — whether Arsenal, it wasn't us — we're the best of thieves, we copied somebody, we then just committed to that. You have to then move with the times to ask, "What's next? How do you innovate?" But yeah, three or four years ago we set out to be actually the best at set pieces, for and against, in the division. It was really cool to do that across Europe.

Innovation and the Entrepreneur in Residence

Charlie: Can I extend that — because you've been excellent in another area? I spend a lot of time trying to convince clubs to open up a little bit more budget to employ tools and technologies that find them a cutting edge in areas where it isn't necessarily geared towards performance — but is doing what we've talked about previously: trying to find those margins around the game. The challenge is it takes investment up front, often before you see return on that.

You've been pretty prominent as a football club — you've had an Entrepreneur in Residence, which for people that don't know is a very un-football job. Really, it's someone with the responsibility of finding and implementing technologies. How have you thought about that and balanced it as a risk?

Liam: I do — and it goes back to the fact that we're almost liberated to be a bit quirky and do something different because of our status and what we're trying to achieve. What happens in football, in English football, in any industry where you benchmark — whether you draw the line at 116 clubs, 72, 92, whichever way — there is then almost a cookie-cutter scenario: "This is what you're supposed to do, this is how you're going to do it."

So when we looked at our whole innovation sphere, that wasn't about trying to get on the front pages of the tech magazines or anything. It was about: how do we shift the dial, and how do we do something different that then net returns into the bank of performance, in terms of investment — how does that help us do something different?

Charlie, the truth: we've had lots of fails in that area.

Charlie: That's interesting.

Liam: Absolutely. But equally, some of the successes that we've had and some of the things we've picked up on — they've made the points of difference. Our biggest challenge in that area was, when we weren't yet seeing any green shoots, to have the commitment to almost double down and then bring in the Entrepreneur in Residence.

I remember when we did that, my biggest concern was, "Oh, that's going to look really knobby." Like, that's going to look like, "Who do they think they are?" And then it went back to: you know what? It is going to help us, it is the right thing to do. So we're going to do it and we'll do it with pride, and everybody can say whatever they want about us — but I'm kind of cool and I'm fine with that.

That was definitely not from a place of, "Oh, let's go and do something different." It was just it worked for us, and we went, "Okay, let's commit and let's do it with pride." The stuff we're starting to see come out of that — and I know I'm talking in abstract terms here — but we've definitely had benefit from that from a commercial point of view already. We've definitely had benefit from a performance point of view already. I can't tell you exactly how much, but we're having benefit, and I'm sure it will go on to help us.

It's almost deliberately not played front and center, just because we do understand that there has to be some level of conformality, we don't want to look like we're trying to break the game — but it is something that works for us in our setting.

Closing

Charlie: Brilliant. Look, Liam — it's so great to have you back in. It's amazing genuinely sitting here, hearing something 20 months ago — hearing that plan laid out, as I said at the beginning — and then to actually see it come to fruition and build up to something now which, even if you may have expected it in October 2024, I'm sure there was a level of reservation. But honestly, a huge huge congratulations to you, to everyone at the club — as you said, full team effort — and I hope for you, for the fans, it's an amazing year next year. So go and enjoy it, and congratulations for operating in a way that clearly is doing a brilliant job in football. Bless you now.

Liam: Thank you very much. Really appreciate your support.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：他们把"接班人计划"放上了桌面
整段对话里最让我"啊哈"的瞬间，不是冠军游行，也不是双主帅。而是 Scully 不带任何隐瞒地承认——他们和主教练 Skubala 一起在酒店里讨论"今晚我去见一个可能接你班的人"，主教练的反应是"那哥们我也想见见"。
足球行业的默认操作系统是**信息保密 + 情感人质**：俱乐部偷偷物色继任者，教练偷偷接听挖角电话，双方互相提防直到爆雷。Lincoln 干脆把这层"地下市场"挪到了地面上。这其实和 **Netflix 的"keeper test"** 是同构的——"如果你今天来面试，我还会雇你吗？"——把组织里最隐蔽的张力变成共享的、透明的对话。它的真正威力不在于"诚实"这种道德感，而在于：**一旦透明化，决策延迟和恐慌成本就归零了**。当 Skubala 真的走向 Bristol City 时，Lincoln 不需要从震惊中恢复——他们早就把这次离别在心里彩排过几十遍了。
## 二、跨领域连接：他们其实在用"工业 OEE"指标管理一支球队
Scully 提了一句被很多人会忽略掉的话——"如果你的工资单都堵在医务室里，
